criteria.name: Accurate
criteria.description: Every claim is either supported by the bill text or is accurate real-world context about the bill's subject (verifiable via general knowledge / web search). Nothing contradicts the bill text or the factual record.

criteria.name: No hallucinations
criteria.description: No fabricated or incorrect claims. Accurate context not stated in the bill — e.g. a program's common name, its administering agency, or the statute being amended — is acceptable and is NOT a hallucination. Only false or invented claims fail this criterion.

criteria.name: States purpose
criteria.description: The summary clearly identifies the bill's primary purpose or subject.

criteria.name: Changes to existing law
criteria.description: The summary describes how the bill amends, repeals, or adds to existing law (e.g. names the statute/program affected), when the bill does so.

criteria.name: Exceptions & conditions
criteria.description: The summary notes key exceptions, conditions, eligibility limits, or carve-outs that are material to the bill.

criteria.name: Effective dates & timing
criteria.description: The summary notes effective dates, sunsets, deadlines, or other timing provisions that the bill specifies.

criteria.name: Major provisions covered
criteria.description: Captures the bill's essential operative effect. A faithful high-level summary is fine; this fails only if a major operative provision is missing or misrepresented — not merely for omitting granular figures or minor sub-provisions.

criteria.name: Objective tone
criteria.description: The summary is neutral and non-partisan, with no advocacy, praise, criticism, or editorializing.

criteria.name: Coherent
criteria.description: The summary is well-organized, internally consistent, and reads clearly.

criteria.name: Concise
criteria.description: The summary avoids padding, repetition, boilerplate, and filler.

criteria.name: Correct entities
criteria.description: Any agencies, officials, programs, or statutes named — including accurate context beyond the bill text — are identified correctly. Adding a correct administering agency or program name is good; naming the wrong one fails.

criteria.name: Correct figures
criteria.description: Any dollar amounts, dates, or quantities the summary states are correct. Omitting figures does not fail this criterion; misstating one does.

bills.title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Energy relating to "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Consumer Gas-fired Instantaneous Water Heaters".

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-6, uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove and nullify a regulation issued by the Department of Energy.

The targeted rule, titled "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Consumer Gas-fired Instantaneous Water Heaters," was published in the Federal Register on December 26, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 105188). This rule had established energy conservation standards for consumer gas-fired instantaneous (tankless) water heaters.

The resolution states that Congress disapproves the rule and that the rule "shall have no force or effect." Under the Congressional Review Act, this means the regulation is treated as though it never took effect.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on February 27, 2025, and by the Senate on April 9 and 10, 2025. It was approved on May 9, 2025.

accurate.why: All claims match the bill text and verified context: the CRA authority, DOE rule, Federal Register citation/date, and legislative dates are correct.

no_hallucinations.why: Added context (Congressional Review Act name, 'tankless' descriptor, CRA never-took-effect treatment) is accurate and verifiable, not fabricated.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the resolution disapproves and nullifies the DOE rule under the CRA.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Correctly explains the resolution nullifies the identified DOE regulation so it has no force or effect.

exceptions_conditions.why: A CRA disapproval resolution contains no material exceptions or carve-outs to summarize.

effective_dates.why: Accurately states House passage (Feb. 27, 2025), Senate passage (Apr. 9-10, 2025), and approval (May 9, 2025).

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the sole operative effect—disapproval and nullification of the named rule under the CRA.

objective_tone.why: Neutral and factual, with no advocacy or editorializing.

coherent.why: Well-organized and internally consistent.

concise.why: Free of padding and repetition while complete.

correct_entities.why: Correctly identifies the Department of Energy, the Congressional Review Act, and the specific rule title.

correct_figures.why: Federal Register citation (89 Fed. Reg. 105188, Dec. 26, 2024) and all dates are accurate.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
This joint resolution disapproves a Department of Energy rule under the Congressional Review Act, chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code. The disapproved rule is titled “Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Consumer Gas-fired Instantaneous Water Heaters” and was published in the Federal Register on December 26, 2024, at 89 Fed. Reg. 105188.

The resolution provides that Congress disapproves the rule and that the rule “shall have no force or effect.” As a result, the Department of Energy’s energy conservation standards for consumer gas-fired instantaneous water heaters contained in that rule do not take effect or remain legally operative.

The resolution contains no additional exceptions, conditions, implementation procedures, or deadlines. It was approved on May 9, 2025.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 119-6 (House Joint Resolution 20) is a joint resolution of Congress that provides for the formal disapproval of a specific federal regulation under chapter 8 of title 5 of the United States Code. Specifically, the resolution targets the rule submitted by the Department of Energy titled "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Consumer Gas-fired Instantaneous Water Heaters," which was published in the Federal Register (89 Fed. Reg. 105188) on December 26, 2024.

The primary purpose and effect of this measure is to nullify the Department of Energy's rule. By declaring congressional disapproval, the resolution mandates that the specified rule shall have no force or effect under the law. This action prevents the energy conservation standards detailed in the December 2024 rule from being implemented or enforced.

The joint resolution was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 27, 2025, and by the Senate on April 9 and 10, 2025. It was signed into law and became effective upon approval on May 9, 2025. The text of the resolution does not contain any exceptions, conditions, or further administrative deadlines.

accurate.why: Accurately describes nullifying the named DOE/IRS/CFTC rule with correct Fed. Reg. citation and dates.

This joint resolution (Public Law 119-6) provides for congressional disapproval of a Department of Energy rule regarding energy conservation standards for consumer gas-fired instantaneous water heaters. The primary purpose of the resolution is to nullify this specific rule, which was submitted by the Department of Energy and published in the Federal Register on December 26, 2024.

Under chapter 8 of title 5 of the United States Code, Congress has the authority to disapprove rules submitted by federal agencies. By enacting this resolution, Congress explicitly disapproves the Department of Energy's rule titled "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Consumer Gas-fired Instantaneous Water Heaters." The resolution's major provision declares that the rule "shall have no force or effect," effectively preventing the energy conservation standards outlined in the rule from being implemented or enforced.

The resolution was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 27, 2025, and by the Senate on April 9 and 10, 2025. It was approved by the President on May 9, 2025. The text contains no additional exceptions, conditions, or deadlines beyond the immediate nullification of the specified rule.

bills.title: Providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Department of Energy relating to "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Walk-In Coolers and Walk-In Freezers".

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-7, exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation issued by the Department of Energy.

Specifically, the resolution disapproves a rule titled "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Walk-In Coolers and Walk-In Freezers," which had been published in the Federal Register at 89 Fed. Reg. 104616 on December 23, 2024. By formally disapproving the rule, Congress provides that the rule "shall have no force or effect," meaning the Department of Energy's energy conservation standards for walk-in coolers and walk-in freezers established by that regulation are nullified and do not take effect.

The resolution passed the House on March 27, 2025, passed the Senate on April 2 and 3, 2025, and was approved on May 9, 2025. Under the Congressional Review Act mechanism it invokes, a rule that is disapproved in this manner may not be reissued in substantially the same form, and a new rule that is substantially the same may not be issued, unless specifically authorized by a subsequent law.

The text of the resolution itself contains no other provisions, exceptions, or conditions beyond the disapproval of the specified rule.

no_hallucinations.why: The Congressional Review Act identification and reissue-prohibition context are accurate and not fabricated.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the resolution disapproves the DOE walk-in coolers/freezers energy conservation rule under the CRA.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Correctly explains the rule is nullified and may not be reissued in substantially the same form absent new law.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes the resolution contains no other provisions or exceptions and the reissuance condition, which is accurate.

effective_dates.why: Accurately states House passage March 27, Senate April 2-3, and approval May 9, 2025.

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the essential operative effect: disapproval and nullification of the specified rule.

objective_tone.why: Neutral and descriptive with no advocacy or editorializing.

coherent.why: Well-organized and internally consistent.

concise.why: Succinct with no significant padding or repetition.

correct_entities.why: Correctly identifies the Department of Energy, the CRA, and the rule title.

correct_figures.why: Federal Register citation (89 Fed. Reg. 104616), dates, and Public Law number all match the bill text.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
This joint resolution eliminates new, more stringent energy conservation standards for certain walk-in coolers and freezers, including the new limits on maximum daily energy consumption for walk-in non-display doors. Under the joint resolution, walk-in non-display doors and walk-in refrigeration systems are no longer required to comply with the new standards.
Specifically, the joint resolution nullifies the rule titled Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Walk-In Coolers and Walk-In Freezers and submitted by the Department of Energy (DOE) on December 23, 2024. Under the rule, DOE adopted energy conservation standards for walk-in coolers and freezers to achieve the maximum improvement in energy efficiency that DOE determined was technologically feasible and economically justified. The rule required walk-in non-display doors to comply with those standards by December 23, 2027, and walk-in refrigeration systems to comply with them by December 31, 2028.

accurate.why: All claims align with the bill (CRA disapproval voiding the DOE rule) and accurate context about the rule verified via the Federal Register and GAO records.

no_hallucinations.why: Contextual details (DOE administration, rule content, deferred compliance dates for non-display doors and refrigeration systems) are accurate real-world facts, not fabrications.

states_purpose.why: Clearly states the resolution nullifies the DOE walk-in coolers/freezers energy conservation standards rule.

changes_to_existing_law.why: Identifies that it nullifies the specific DOE rule (89 FR 104616) and describes the standards being voided.

exceptions_conditions.why: A one-line CRA disapproval has no material exceptions or carve-outs to note.

effective_dates.why: Notes the rule's submission date (Dec 23, 2024) and the rule's compliance deadlines, consistent with the rule's deferred dates.

major_provisions_covered.why: Captures the essential operative effect: the rule is disapproved and has no force or effect.

objective_tone.why: Neutral and descriptive with no advocacy or editorializing.

coherent.why: Well-organized and internally consistent.

concise.why: Compact with no padding or boilerplate.

correct_entities.why: Correctly names the Department of Energy and the precise rule title.

correct_figures.why: The submission date (Dec 23, 2024) is correct and the stated compliance deadlines for non-display doors and refrigeration systems match the rule's deferred dates.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation.

Specifically, Congress disapproves a rule issued by the Internal Revenue Service titled "Gross Proceeds Reporting by Brokers That Regularly Provide Services Effectuating Digital Asset Sales," which was published in the Federal Register on December 30, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 106928). The IRS rule had established tax reporting requirements for brokers involved in digital asset sales.

Under the resolution, the IRS rule "shall have no force or effect," meaning the regulation is nullified and will not be implemented or enforced.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on March 11, 2025, passed by the Senate on March 26, 2025, and approved (signed into law) on April 10, 2025, becoming Public Law 119-5.

z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
Public Law 119-5 (H.J. Res. 25) is a joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval of a specific Internal Revenue Service (IRS) rule under chapter 8 of title 5 of the United States Code. The primary purpose of the law is to nullify the IRS rule titled "Gross Proceeds Reporting by Brokers That Regularly Provide Services Effectuating Digital Asset Sales," which was published in the Federal Register on December 30, 2024. 

Under the resolution, Congress formally disapproves the rule. As a result, the rule is stripped of its legal force and effect, meaning it cannot be implemented or enforced. This action utilizes the congressional review mechanism, which allows Congress to overturn federal agency rules. 

The law was approved on April 10, 2025. It was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on March 11, 2025, and by the Senate on March 26, 2025. The resolution contains no additional provisions, exceptions, or conditions beyond the direct nullification of the specified IRS rule.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code), nullifies a final rule issued by the Internal Revenue Service. The rule, titled “Gross Proceeds Reporting by Brokers That Regularly Provide Services Effectuating Digital Asset Sales,” was published in the Federal Register on December 30, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 106928). By passing this resolution, Congress disapproves the rule in its entirety and declares that it shall have no force or effect.

The resolution does not replace the rule with new requirements, create exceptions, or set future effective dates. It simply eliminates the regulation from operation. The measure completed its legislative path on April 10, 2025, when it was approved and became Public Law 119-5.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
This joint resolution nullifies requirements for persons effectuating decentralized financial (DeFi) transactions to report certain information regarding digital asset sales to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Specifically, the joint resolution nullifies the requirements included in the rule titled Gross Proceeds Reporting by Brokers That Regularly Provide Services Effectuating Digital Asset Sales and issued by the IRS on December 30, 2024. 
Decentralized finance refers to the suite of financial activities and services that are facilitated by cryptocurrency and intended to be conducted without any sort of reliance on traditional financial tools or intermediaries.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-2, uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation issued by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

The specific rule disapproved is titled "Waste Emissions Charge for Petroleum and Natural Gas Systems: Procedures for Facilitating Compliance, Including Netting and Exemptions," which the EPA published in the Federal Register on November 18, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 91094). This rule established procedures related to a charge on waste emissions (such as methane) from petroleum and natural gas systems, including provisions for netting and exemptions.

Under the resolution, Congress formally disapproves of this rule, and the rule "shall have no force or effect." This means the EPA regulation is nullified and cannot be implemented or enforced.

The resolution was passed by the House on February 26, 2025, passed by the Senate on February 27, 2025, and was approved (signed into law) on March 14, 2025. The law takes effect upon approval. The text contains no additional provisions, exceptions, or conditions beyond the disapproval of the identified rule.

Public Law 119-2, enacted as House Joint Resolution 35, serves the primary purpose of nullifying a specific regulation issued by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). Under the authority of chapter 8 of title 5 of the United States Code, which governs congressional review of agency rulemaking, Congress formally disapproves the EPA rule titled "Waste Emissions Charge for Petroleum and Natural Gas Systems: Procedures for Facilitating Compliance, Including Netting and Exemptions." 

This disapproved rule was originally published in the Federal Register on November 18, 2024, under the citation 89 Fed. Reg. 91094. By enacting this joint resolution, Congress dictates that the specified EPA rule shall have no force or effect under law, effectively preventing its implementation or enforcement. 

The legislative history of the resolution indicates that it was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on February 26, 2025, and subsequently considered and passed by the Senate on February 27, 2025. The joint resolution was officially approved and signed into law on March 14, 2025. There are no exceptions, conditions, or future deadlines specified in the text of the resolution.

Under the Congressional Review Act, Congress has the authority to disapprove federal agency rules through a joint resolution. This resolution states that Congress disapproves the EPA’s rule in its entirety, and the rule “shall have no force or effect.” As a result, the rule is void and cannot be enforced or implemented.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-8, uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation issued by the Department of Energy.

The specific rule disapproved is titled "Energy Conservation Program for Appliance Standards: Certification Requirements, Labeling Requirements, and Enforcement Provisions for Certain Consumer Products and Commercial Equipment," which was published in the Federal Register at 89 Fed. Reg. 81994 on October 9, 2024.

The resolution states that Congress disapproves the rule and that the rule "shall have no force or effect." As a result, the Department of Energy regulation does not take effect and is treated as though it had never gone into force. Under the Congressional Review Act, this disapproval also generally bars the agency from issuing a substantially similar rule in the future unless authorized by subsequent legislation.

The measure passed the House of Representatives on March 5, 2025, and the Senate on April 29 and 30, 2025. It was approved on May 9, 2025.

The resolution contains no provisions beyond nullifying the identified rule; it does not establish replacement standards or other new requirements.

crs_reference.summary:
This joint resolution eliminates new, more stringent certification, labeling, and enforcement requirements related to energy conservation standards for specific types of appliances.
Specifically, the joint resolution nullifies a rule titled the Energy Conservation Program for Appliance Standards: Certification Requirements, Labeling Requirements, and Enforcement Provisions for Certain Consumer Products and Commercial Equipment and published by the Department of Energy (DOE) on October 9, 2024. Under the rule, DOE modified its regulations on the energy efficiency of certain types of consumer products (e.g., washing machines and dishwashers) and industrial equipment (e.g., computer room air conditioners). DOE modified certification requirements, labeling requirements, and enforcement provisions for these products and equipment to (1) align reporting requirements with currently applicable energy conservation standards and test procedures, and (2) provide DOE with the information necessary to determine the appropriate classification of products for the application of standards.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-13, uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation issued by an executive agency.

Specifically, the resolution disapproves a rule submitted by the National Park Service of the Department of the Interior titled "Glen Canyon National Recreation Area: Motor Vehicles," which was published in the Federal Register at 90 Fed. Reg. 2621. As a result of this disapproval, the rule "shall have no force or effect," meaning it does not take effect and cannot be enforced.

The resolution was passed by the House of Representatives on April 29, 2025, and by the Senate on May 6 and 8, 2025. It was approved (signed into law) on May 23, 2025.

The text of the resolution does not describe the contents of the underlying rule, nor does it establish any new regulatory provisions of its own. Its sole legal effect is to nullify the identified National Park Service rule regarding motor vehicles in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 119-13 (House Joint Resolution 60), enacted on May 23, 2025, serves to formally disapprove a federal rule issued by the National Park Service of the Department of the Interior. Specifically, the joint resolution targets the administrative rule titled "Glen Canyon National Recreation Area: Motor Vehicles," which was published in the Federal Register at 90 Fed. Reg. 2621. 

Under the authority of Chapter 8 of Title 5 of the United States Code, which governs congressional review of agency rulemaking, the resolution declares that Congress disapproves of this regulation. As a direct result of this legislative action, the specified rule is stripped of its legal authority and shall have no force or effect. 

The joint resolution contains no exceptions, conditions, or future deadlines, meaning the nullification of the rule is absolute and effective immediately upon the law's enactment. According to the legislative history, the resolution was considered and passed by the House of Representatives on April 29, 2025, and by the Senate on May 8, 2025, before receiving final presidential approval on May 23, 2025.

This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-13, exercises Congress's authority under chapter 8 of title 5 of the United States Code to disapprove a federal rule. Specifically, the resolution targets a rule submitted by the National Park Service, an agency within the Department of the Interior, relating to the management and use of motor vehicles in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area. The rule in question was published in the Federal Register at 90 Fed. Reg. 2621.

The primary purpose of the legislation is to formally express Congress's disapproval of this National Park Service rule. To give effect to this disapproval, the resolution's sole substantive provision declares that the rule "shall have no force or effect." This action effectively nullifies the specified rule, preventing it from being implemented or enforced by the Department of the Interior.

The resolution does not include any exceptions, conditions, or alternative regulatory frameworks; it simply voids the targeted rule. The legislative history indicates that the House of Representatives considered and passed the joint resolution on April 29, 2025, and the Senate considered and passed it on May 6 and 8, 2025. The resolution was subsequently approved on May 23, 2025, at which point it became law.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
This joint resolution allows off-road vehicles to be used in certain areas of the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area, which is in northern Arizona and southeastern Utah and contains Lake Powell.
Specifically, the joint resolution nullifies the rule issued by the National Park Service (NPS) titled Glen Canyon National Recreation Area; Motor Vehicles and published on January 13, 2025. Under the rule, the NPS limited the use of off-road vehicles, such as off-highway vehicles and all-terrain vehicles, in certain areas. For instance, the rule prohibited the use of off-road vehicles on an 8-mile segment of the Poison Spring Loop located on Route 633 proceeding north to Route 730 in the Orange Cliffs Special Management Unit. The rule also eliminated the authority of the NPS to open the upper portion of the Flint Trail in that unit to off-road vehicles. Additionally, the rule limited off-road vehicle use from certain roads to the shoreline of the lake.

accurate.why: Correctly describes nullifying the NPS Glen Canyon off-road vehicle rule; geography (AZ/UT, Lake Powell) and route details are accurate context.

no_hallucinations.why: Specific routes (Poison Spring Loop, Flint Trail) are drawn from the rule, not invented.

effective_dates.why: No timing provisions.

objective_tone.why: Neutral.

coherent.why: Clear.

concise.why: Detailed but not padded.

correct_entities.why: NPS correct.

correct_figures.why: No standalone figures requiring verification.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 119-9 (H.J. Res. 75), approved on May 9, 2025, is a joint resolution of Congress. Its primary purpose is to formally disapprove a federal rule submitted by the Office of Energy Efficiency and Renewable Energy, a division of the Department of Energy. 

The specific rule targeted by this resolution is titled "Energy Conservation Program: Energy Conservation Standards for Commercial Refrigerators, Freezers, and Refrigerator-Freezers," which was published in the Federal Register on January 21, 2025 (90 Fed. Reg. 7464). 

Under the authority of chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, the major provision of this joint resolution is the explicit disapproval of this rule. By enacting this resolution, Congress changes the status of the law by rendering the Department of Energy's rule entirely void, declaring that the rule shall have no force or effect. 

The resolution contains no material exceptions, conditions, or future deadlines. Its mandate is absolute and took effect immediately upon its approval on May 9, 2025, preventing the energy conservation standards detailed in the rule from having any legal standing or enforcement power.

accurate.why: Accurately describes nullifying the named DOE/IRS/CFTC rule with correct Fed. Reg. citation and dates.

Reg. 7464). 

The primary purpose of the resolution is to formally reject the Department of Energy's energy conservation standards for commercial refrigeration equipment. The major provision of the law states that Congress disapproves of the rule and that the rule shall have no force or effect. anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove and nullify a specific rule issued by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC).

The rule at issue is the CFTC's final guidance titled "Commission Guidance Regarding the Listing of Voluntary Carbon Credit Derivative Contracts," published in the Federal Register on October 15, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 83378).

If enacted, the resolution declares that Congress disapproves the guidance and that the rule "shall have no force or effect." Under the Congressional Review Act, this means the guidance would be treated as though it had never taken effect, and the agency would generally be barred from reissuing the rule in substantially the same form unless specifically authorized by a subsequent law.

The resolution contains no other provisions, exceptions, or effective dates beyond the disapproval itself. It was introduced in the House of Representatives on April 3, 2025, by Mrs. Bice and referred to the Committee on Agriculture.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary: This joint resolution nullifies a Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) final guidance document concerning the listing of voluntary carbon credit derivative contracts. The guidance was published in the Federal Register on October 15, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 83378). Under the terms of the resolution, Congress disapproves the guidance, and it will have no force or effect. The resolution is introduced under the Congressional Review Act, which provides a fast-track process for Congress to overturn agency rules. If enacted, the CFTC guidance would be treated as though it never took effect, and the agency would be barred from issuing a substantially similar rule in the future without subsequent statutory authorization. The resolution does not include any exceptions, conditions, or delayed effective dates beyond the immediate disapproval.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove and nullify a rule issued by the Bureau of Land Management.

The specific rule being disapproved is the "Miles City Field Office Record of Decision and Approved Resource Management Plan Amendment," which the Bureau of Land Management issued on November 20, 2024. The resolution notes that the Government Accountability Office, in a letter dated June 25, 2025 (printed in the Congressional Record on June 26, 2025), concluded that this record of decision and resource management plan amendment qualifies as a "rule" subject to the Congressional Review Act.

Under the resolution, Congress formally disapproves the rule, and the rule "shall have no force or effect." This means the BLM action is invalidated and cannot be implemented.

The resolution was passed by the House on September 3, 2025, passed by the Senate on October 7 and 8, 2025, and was approved (signed into law) on December 11, 2025, becoming Public Law 119-48.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 119-48 (House Joint Resolution 104) is a joint resolution of Congress that disapproves a specific rule issued by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM). Under the authority of the Congressional Review Act (Chapter 8 of Title 5, United States Code), the resolution declares that the disapproved BLM rule shall have no force or effect. 

The rule targeted by the resolution is titled "Miles City Field Office Record of Decision and Approved Resource Management Plan Amendment." This agency action was originally issued on November 20, 2024, as a record of decision and resource management plan amendment. Its classification as a "rule" subject to congressional disapproval under the Congressional Review Act was established by a Government Accountability Office (GAO) letter of opinion dated June 25, 2025, which was subsequently printed in the Congressional Record on June 26, 2025. 

The joint resolution passed the House of Representatives on September 3, 2025, and the Senate on October 8, 2025. It was approved by the President and became law on December 11, 2025.

This joint resolution (Public Law 119-48) exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove of a specific rule submitted by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM). The primary purpose of the resolution is to nullify the BLM's "Miles City Field Office Record of Decision and Approved Resource Management Plan Amendment," which was issued on November 20, 2024.

The resolution references a letter of opinion from the Government Accountability Office (GAO) dated June 25, 2025, which concluded that the BLM's record of decision and resource management plan amendment qualifies as a "rule" subject to the Congressional Review Act. 

The resolution's sole provision declares that Congress disapproves of the BLM rule and stipulates that the rule "shall have no force or effect." This action effectively repeals the rule, preventing it from being implemented or enforced. 

Legislatively, the House of Representatives considered and passed the resolution on September 3, 2025, and the Senate considered and passed it on October 7 and 8, 2025. The resolution was approved and became law on December 11, 2025. The text includes no material exceptions or conditions beyond the nullification of the specified rule.

bills.title: Requesting the Secretary of the Interior to authorize unique and one-time arrangements for displays on the National Mall and the Washington Monument during the period beginning on December 31, 2025, and ending on January 5, 2026.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution requests that the Secretary of the Interior authorize special, one-time arrangements for displays on the National Mall and the Washington Monument to help launch celebrations of the 250th anniversary (semiquincentennial) of the United States' founding.

The resolution's preamble recounts the historical context, including the adoption of the Declaration of Independence on July 4, 1776, the upcoming 250th anniversary on July 4, 2026, the creation of the United States Semiquincentennial Commission under the 2016 Commission Act, and prior precedents such as the 1976 bicentennial lighting of capital monuments and the 2019 Apollo 11 50th anniversary commemoration that projected footage onto the Washington Monument.

Specifically, the resolution requests that the Secretary of the Interior authorize unique and one-time arrangements for displaying semiquincentennial materials and other significant artifacts, digital content, film footage, and associated audio and imagery in and around the National Mall, including projection onto the surface of the Washington Monument, for five nights of public display during the period from December 31, 2025, through January 5, 2026.

The resolution also requests that the Clerk of the House of Representatives transmit an enrolled copy of the resolution to the Secretary of the Interior and to the Chair of the United States Semiquincentennial Commission.

This measure is a request rather than a mandate; it expresses Congress's wishes but does not by its terms compel the Secretary to act or create new legal authority. It was passed by the House on November 18, 2025, by the Senate on November 20, 2025, and approved on December 2, 2025, as Public Law 119-46.

It references prior congressional action creating the United States Semiquincentennial Commission and notes earlier commemorative displays on the National Mall, including the 2019 Apollo 11 anniversary projection on the Washington Monument.

The resolution also requests that the Clerk of the House transmit an enrolled copy of the resolution to the Secretary of the Interior and to the Chair of the United States Semiquincentennial Commission. It does not amend existing law or establish a continuing program; the requested arrangements are described as unique and one-time.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Public Law 119-46, a joint resolution approved on December 2, 2025, requests the Secretary of the Interior to authorize unique, one-time arrangements for public displays on the National Mall and the Washington Monument. The primary purpose of these displays is to initiate celebrations for the United States semiquincentennial, which marks the 250th anniversary of the founding of the United States.

Specifically, the resolution requests the authorization of displays featuring United States semiquincentennial materials, significant artifacts, digital content, film footage, and associated audio and imagery. These displays are to be located in and around the vicinity of the National Mall, including projections onto the surface of the Washington Monument. 

The resolution establishes a specific timeframe for the event, requesting that the displays run for five nights during the period beginning on December 31, 2025, and ending on January 5, 2026. This period is intended to mark the transition into the country's 250th year, leading up to the official anniversary on July 4, 2026. 

Additionally, the resolution requests that the Clerk of the House of Representatives transmit enrolled copies of the measure to the Secretary of the Interior and the Chair of the United States Semiquincentennial Commission.

The permitted display would include United States semiquincentennial materials, other significant artifacts, digital content, film footage, and associated audio and imagery. The projection of these materials onto the surface of the Washington Monument is specifically mentioned.

Beyond the request to the Interior Secretary, the resolution also asks the Clerk of the House of Representatives to send an enrolled copy of the resolution to the Secretary and to the Chair of the United States Semiquincentennial Commission. The preamble references the bicentennial and a 2019 Apollo 11 anniversary event on the Mall as precedents, but those historical references do not create legal requirements. No new statutory authority or permanent change to law is made.

This joint resolution would terminate a specific national emergency previously declared by the President. The emergency in question was declared on July 30, 2025, through Executive Order 14323 (published at 90 Fed. Reg. 37739), and was used as the basis for imposing duties (tariffs) on goods imported from Brazil.

Acting under section 202 of the National Emergencies Act (50 U.S.C. 1622), which gives Congress the authority to end a declared national emergency through a joint resolution, this measure declares that the national emergency is terminated.

The resolution contains no additional provisions, conditions, exceptions, or specified effective dates beyond the termination itself. Its sole effect is to end the referenced national emergency.

House Joint Resolution 147 is a joint resolution introduced in the United States House of Representatives on February 2, 2026. The primary purpose of the resolution is to terminate the national emergency that was declared to impose duties on articles imported into the United States from Brazil. 

To achieve this, the resolution's sole major provision states that, pursuant to section 202 of the National Emergencies Act (50 U.S.C. 1622), the national emergency declared by the President on July 30, 2025, in Executive Order 14323 (90 Fed. Reg. 37739) is officially terminated. By ending this emergency declaration, the resolution would remove the legal framework established under that executive order for levying tariffs on Brazilian imports. 

The resolution was introduced by Representative Gregory Meeks, along with co-sponsors Representatives Kamlager-Dove, Castro of Texas, Titus, Stanton, Amo, and Casar, and has been referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs. The text of the resolution does not outline any material exceptions, conditions, or specific future effective dates, indicating that the termination of the declared national emergency would take effect immediately upon the enactment of the joint resolution.

bills.title: To direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba that have not been authorized by Congress.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced in the House of Representatives in the 119th Congress, directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba that Congress has not authorized.

The resolution begins with findings stating that the Constitution gives Congress the sole power to declare war, while the President has a responsibility to defend the United States and its citizens, service members, and diplomats from attack. It notes that Congress has not declared war on Cuba or enacted any specific statutory authorization for the use of military force within or against Cuba. The findings characterize the use of force against Cuba—including using the Coast Guard and other Armed Forces components to conduct a blockade or quarantine—as introducing U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under section 4(a) of the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Cuba, unless such action is explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force. This directive is invoked under section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and is to be considered under the expedited procedures of section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it may be interpreted to prevent the United States from defending itself against an armed attack or the threat of an imminent armed attack, or from lawfully carrying out counternarcotics operations.

The resolution does not specify a particular effective date or deadline for the removal of forces beyond the directive itself.

This joint resolution would direct the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Republic of Cuba unless Congress has explicitly authorized those hostilities through a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force.

The resolution states Congress’s findings that Congress has the constitutional power to declare war, while the President has responsibility to defend the United States, its territories, possessions, citizens, service members, and diplomats from attack. It further states that Congress has not declared war on Cuba or on any person or organization within Cuba, nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for the use of military force within or against Cuba. The findings also state that using U.S. Armed Forces within or against Cuba, including use of the Coast Guard or other Armed Forces components to conduct a blockade or quarantine of Cuba, constitutes the introduction of U.S. forces into hostilities under the War Powers Resolution.

The resolution relies on existing provisions of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976, which provide expedited procedures for measures requiring removal of U.S. forces from hostilities or imminent hostilities without congressional authorization.

The directive would not prevent the United States from defending itself against an armed attack or the threat of an imminent armed attack. It also would not prevent the lawful execution of counternarcotics operations. The resolution does not specify a separate effective date or deadline for removal.

bills.title: Investing in All of America Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Investing in All of America Act of 2025 (Public Law 119-92) amends the Small Business Investment Act of 1958 to change the rules governing leverage available to Small Business Investment Companies (SBICs)—privately owned investment firms licensed by the Small Business Administration (SBA).

The bill revises the definition of "private capital" in the Act. It removes a requirement that certain qualifying institutional investments be "established prior to October 1, 1987," and expands eligible sources to include foundations, endowments, or trusts of colleges or universities. It also clarifies that private capital does not include funds obtained directly or indirectly from federal, state, or local governments (with limited exceptions) when the Administrator considers leverage requests.

The bill restructures the maximum leverage limits for SBICs. For a single company, it lowers one baseline leverage cap from $300 million to $200 million, and sets new caps of $250 million for companies making quarterly or semiannual interest payments and $175 million for other companies licensed under section 301(c). For companies under common control, it replaces the prior $350 million limit with $475 million for those making quarterly or semiannual interest payments and $350 million for others.

The bill broadens an exclusion from leverage calculations for investments in certain businesses. Previously focused on low-income geographic areas, the exclusion now also covers small business concerns in rural areas, businesses operating primarily in a "covered technology category" (as defined in title 10), and small manufacturers. The aggregate amount that may be excluded for a company or commonly controlled companies is capped at the lesser of 50 percent of their private capital or $125 million.

The exclusion applies only prospectively: an investment qualifies only if made after the date of enactment of the relevant provision.

The Act was approved on May 19, 2026.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Investing in All of America Act of 2025 amends the Small Business Investment Act of 1958 to change how leverage limits are calculated for small business investment companies (SBICs), especially for investments in specified types of businesses and areas.

The Act revises the definition of “private capital” used for SBIC leverage approvals. It removes a date restriction on certain government-related pension plan funds, expands covered sources to include foundations, endowments, or trusts of colleges and universities, and provides that funds obtained directly or indirectly from federal, state, or local governments or their agencies generally may not count as private capital for leverage approval, except for specified categories already included in the statute.

The Act changes maximum leverage limits for SBICs. It reduces the general leverage-to-private-capital percentage limit from 300 percent to 200 percent. It sets the dollar leverage cap at $250 million for an SBIC that makes quarterly or semiannual interest payments, and $175 million for other licensed companies. For commonly controlled SBICs, it raises the aggregate dollar cap to $475 million if they make quarterly or semiannual interest payments, while retaining a $350 million cap for other commonly controlled companies.

The Act also expands and revises an exclusion from the calculation of outstanding leverage. Investments may be excluded when made in small business concerns located in low-income geographic areas or rural areas, small business concerns operating primarily in covered technology categories, or small manufacturers. The exclusion applies to calculations for both individual and commonly controlled company leverage limits. However, the excluded amount may not exceed the lesser of 50 percent of the company’s or companies’ private capital or $125 million, and the 200 percent leverage limit must still be maintained. Only investments made after May 19, 2026, the date of enactment, are eligible for this exclusion.

The "Investing in All of America Act of 2025" amends the Small Business Investment Act of 1958 to modify leverage limits and capital definitions for Small Business Investment Companies (SBICs). 

First, the Act updates the definition of private capital. It includes college or university foundations, endowments, or trusts under certain funding provisions and removes an age restriction on eligible pension plans. It also specifies that private capital does not include funds obtained directly or indirectly from federal, state, or local government entities, except for certain designated funds.

Second, the Act adjusts maximum leverage limits. It reduces a key leverage multiplier from 300 percent to 200 percent of private capital. It sets the maximum leverage for a single company at $250 million if the company makes quarterly or semiannual interest payments, and $175 million for other licensed companies. For commonly controlled companies, the maximum leverage is capped at $475 million for those making quarterly or semiannual interest payments, and $350 million for others.

Third, the bill allows SBICs to exclude certain investments from their outstanding leverage calculations. This exclusion applies to investments made in small businesses located in low-income geographic or rural areas, small businesses operating primarily in covered technology categories, and small manufacturers. 

This leverage exclusion is subject to specific conditions. The aggregate excluded amount for a company or companies cannot exceed the lesser of 50 percent of their private capital or $125 million. Additionally, this exclusion applies prospectively, meaning it only covers eligible investments made after the bill's enactment on May 19, 2026.

accurate.why: Accurate and detailed; the $475M commonly-controlled cap and all other figures match the bill text (CRS's reference erroneously said $450M).

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Investing in All of America Act of 2025 amends the Small Business Investment Act of 1958 to exclude certain investments by Small Business Investment Companies (SBICs) from their maximum leverage limits. Specifically, the bill excludes investments in small businesses located in rural or low-income geographic areas, small businesses operating in critical technology areas, and small manufacturers.

The bill modifies the definition of "private capital" to exclude funds obtained directly or indirectly from federal, state, or local governments, with exceptions for certain pension plans and university endowments or trusts. It also adjusts the maximum leverage limits for SBICs. The base leverage limit for a single company is lowered from $300 million to $200 million, with specific limits set at $250 million for companies making quarterly or semiannual interest payments and $175 million for others. For commonly controlled companies, the aggregate limit is $475 million if they make such interest payments, or $350 million otherwise.

For the expanded exclusion, the aggregate amount excluded from the leverage calculation may not exceed the lesser of 50 percent of the company’s private capital or $125 million. The exclusion applies prospectively, meaning only investments made by a licensee after the date of enactment are eligible. The act was approved on May 19, 2026.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Investing in All of America Act of 2025 amends the Small Business Investment Act of 1958 to modify the leverage limits for Small Business Investment Companies (SBICs) and to broaden the categories of investments that may be excluded from those limits. The law also revises the definition of “financing” for SBICs.

First, the law adjusts SBIC maximum leverage numbers. The general individual company leverage cap is lowered from $300 million to $200 million. A higher cap of $250 million is set for companies that make quarterly or semiannual interest payments, while other companies licensed under section 301(c) have a $175 million cap. For groups of commonly controlled companies, the combined maximum is raised from $350 million to $475 million for those that make periodic interest payments; for others the combined maximum remains $350 million.

Second, the law significantly expands the types of investments that SBICs can exclude when calculating outstanding leverage. Previously, investments in small businesses in low-income geographic areas could be excluded. The law adds investments in: small businesses located in rural areas (as defined in the Agricultural Act of 1961); small business concerns operating primarily in a covered technology category (as defined in Title 10 of the U.S. Code); and small manufacturers (as defined in the Small Business Investment Act). The total amount a company or group of companies may exclude is limited to the lesser of 50 percent of their private capital or $125 million. This exclusion applies only to investments made after the date of enactment, May 19, 2026.

Finally, the law amends the definition of “financing” to specify that, for purposes of the Small Business Administration’s approval of any request for leverage, the term does not include funds obtained directly or indirectly from any Federal, state, or local government or government agency, except for certain institutional funds such as pension plans, college or university foundations, endowments, or trusts. A separate technical change removes a date restriction related to such institutional sources.

accurate.why: The summary twice states the bill revises/amends the definition of "financing," but section 103(9) (15 U.S.C. 662(9)) actually defines "private capital," so this claim contradicts the bill text.

no_hallucinations.why: Claiming the bill revises the definition of "financing" is an incorrect claim; the term defined and amended in section 103(9) is "private capital."

exceptions_conditions.why: The summary notes the key conditions: the lesser-of-50%-or-$125M exclusion cap, prospective applicability, and the government-funds carve-out with institutional exceptions.

effective_dates.why: The summary correctly notes the exclusion applies only to investments made after the enactment date, May 19, 2026.

major_provisions_covered.why: The summary captures the essential operative effects: revised leverage caps, expanded exclusion categories, the exclusion cap, and the government-funds provision.

objective_tone.why: The summary is neutral and descriptive with no advocacy or editorializing.

concise.why: The summary is appropriately concise without padding or repetition.

correct_figures.why: All figures—$300M to $200M, $250M, $175M, $350M to $475M, $125M, 50%, and the May 19, 2026 date—match the bill text.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Investing in All of America Act of 2025
This act modifies the limit on the amount of financing available to a Small Business Investment Company (SBIC) from the Small Business Administration (SBA). It also expands the definition of private capital with respect to SBICs.
Specifically, the act reduces the maximum outstanding financing available to an SBIC from 300% to 200% of the SBIC's private capital. The act increases from $350 million to $450 million the maximum financing available to two or more commonly controlled SBICs that make quarterly or semiannual interest payments.
The act also expands the amounts that may be excluded from the calculation of the financing limit to include the amounts an SBIC invests in (1) rural areas, (2) certain technology categories, or (3) small manufacturers. The act revises the cap on such excluded amounts to the lesser of $125 million or the aggregate of 50% of the private capital of the SBIC.
Additionally, the act expands what is considered the private capital of an SBIC to include funds obtained from the business revenue of additional government-sponsored corporations and funds invested by the trust or endowment of a college or university.

bills.title: Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act" (H.R. 2427) would prohibit price gouging during federally declared major disasters or emergencies and classify such conduct as an unfair and deceptive act or practice.

After the President declares a major disaster or emergency under sections 401 or 501 of the Robert T. Stafford Disaster Relief and Emergency Assistance Act, and within the declared area, the bill bars a person from raising prices more than 10 percent above the price charged the day before the declaration. This limit applies for 30 days to essential consumer goods and services, hotel lodging, and residential rentals, and for 180 days to repair or reconstruction services. It also bars charging a price more than 50 percent above the seller's cost for 30 days if that price was not charged before the declaration.

Exceptions apply when an increase is directly attributable to higher supplier, labor, or material costs (capped at 10 percent above cost plus the seller's customary markup), or to tariffs or national trade policies. Exceptions also cover regularly scheduled seasonal hotel/motel rate adjustments and rent increases tied to non-routine repairs or contractually agreed terms predating the disaster.

The bill defines "essential consumer goods and services" to include food, emergency and medical supplies, heating oil, construction materials, transportation, freight, storage, and fuel.

Enforcement: Violations are treated as violations of a Federal Trade Commission Act rule on unfair or deceptive practices, enforceable by the FTC. State attorneys general may sue on behalf of residents, with notice to the FTC, which may intervene; states cannot proceed against defendants named in a pending federal action. Injured individuals may bring private suits for injunctions and actual damages, with up to triple damages for willful violations, plus costs and attorney's fees, within two years of discovery.

Civil penalties are up to $25,000 per violation, with total liability for related acts capped at $25,000, adjusted annually for inflation. Recovered amounts fund affected disaster communities. The Act does not preempt non-conflicting state laws.

objective_tone.why: Neutral and descriptive with no editorializing.

coherent.why: Well-organized and internally consistent.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act would prohibit certain price increases in areas covered by a presidential major disaster or emergency declaration under the Stafford Act, treating violations as unfair or deceptive acts or practices enforceable by the Federal Trade Commission.

After such a declaration, a person in the declared area could not increase prices by more than 10 percent above the price charged the day before the declaration for essential consumer goods and services, hotel lodging, and residential rental properties for 30 days, or for repair or reconstruction services for 180 days. For 30 days after the declaration, a person who did not previously charge a price for essential consumer goods and services, hotel lodging, residential rental property, or reconstruction services could not charge a price more than 50 percent greater than that person’s cost.

The bill provides exceptions where the increase is directly attributable to increased supplier, labor, or materials costs and is no more than 10 percent above the seller’s cost plus customary pre-disaster markup; where the increase is directly attributable to tariffs or national trade policies; for hotel or motel rates based on regularly scheduled seasonal adjustments; and for rental increases attributable to amortized repair or addition costs beyond normal maintenance or to increases contractually agreed to before the disaster or emergency.

Violations would be enforced by the FTC under its existing authorities. Civil penalties would be up to $25,000 per violation, with total penalties for the same or related acts or practices capped at $25,000, adjusted annually for inflation beginning after the first Consumer Price Index publication at least one year after enactment. Recovered penalties would go to a fund assisting affected communities.

State attorneys general or other state officials could bring civil actions on behalf of residents, subject to notice to the FTC and limits when a federal action is pending. Injured persons could also sue for injunctions and actual monetary losses, with possible treble damages for willful violations, costs, and attorney’s fees. Private actions must be filed within two years of discovery or reasonable opportunity to discover the violation. The bill would not preempt nonconflicting state laws.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act" (H.R. 2427) prohibits price gouging in areas affected by a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency. 

Specifically, the bill bars sellers from increasing prices by more than 10% compared to the day before the disaster declaration. This restriction applies for 30 days to hotel lodging, residential rental properties, and essential consumer goods and services—including food, emergency and medical supplies, fuel, and transportation—and for 180 days to repair or reconstruction services. Additionally, for 30 days post-declaration, sellers cannot charge a price more than 50% greater than their cost if they did not charge that price beforehand.

Exceptions are permitted if the price increase is directly caused by tariffs, national trade policies, or increased supplier, labor, or material costs (provided the price does not exceed the cost plus 10% more than the seller's customary markup). Regularly scheduled seasonal hotel rate adjustments and certain amortized rental repair costs or pre-existing rental agreements are also exempt.

The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) enforces violations as unfair or deceptive practices. State attorneys general may bring civil actions on behalf of residents, and injured individuals may file private lawsuits within two years of discovering a violation, with courts authorized to award up to treble damages for willful violations. Civil penalties are capped at $25,000 per violation and $25,000 in total for related practices, adjusted annually for inflation. Recovered penalties will fund assistance for communities in declared disaster zones. The Act does not preempt non-conflicting state laws.

accurate.why: Thorough, accurate account of the price-gouging caps, exceptions, enforcement, and penalties.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act prohibits price gouging during a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency. Within the declared area, for 30 days after the declaration, individuals and businesses may not increase prices by more than 10 percent for essential consumer goods and services, hotel lodging, and residential rental properties compared to the day before the declaration. For repair or reconstruction services, this 10 percent cap lasts for 180 days. Additionally, for 30 days, sellers may not charge a price exceeding 50 percent greater than their cost if they did not charge that price prior to the declaration.

Exceptions apply if price increases are directly attributable to additional supplier, labor, or material costs (not exceeding customary markup plus 10 percent), tariffs, or national trade policies. Seasonal hotel rate adjustments and certain pre-agreed or cost-justified rental increases are also exempt.

The bill grants enforcement authority to the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), treating violations as unfair or deceptive practices under the FTC Act. State attorneys general may bring civil actions on behalf of residents, provided they notify the FTC and no overlapping federal action is pending. The bill also creates a private right of action, allowing injured individuals to sue for injunctions or actual monetary damages within two years of discovering the violation. Courts may award treble damages for willful violations and must award attorney's fees and costs to prevailing plaintiffs.

Civil penalties are capped at $25,000 per person for related violations, with annual inflation adjustments. Collected penalties are deposited into a fund for disaster-affected communities. The Act does not preempt state laws that do not conflict with it. Essential consumer goods and services are defined to include food, emergency and medical supplies, building materials, transportation, fuel, and storage.

accurate.why: Thorough, accurate account of the price-gouging caps, exceptions, FTC/state/private enforcement, penalties, and fund.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, titled the “Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act,” makes it an unfair or deceptive practice to sharply raise prices on certain goods and services after a presidentially declared major disaster or emergency. It applies within the declared disaster area and takes effect on the date of the declaration.

During the first 30 days after the declaration, sellers may not raise the price of essential consumer goods and services, hotel lodging, or residential rental properties by more than 10 percent above the price charged the day before the declaration. For repair or reconstruction services, this 10-percent cap lasts 180 days. Additionally, for 30 days after the declaration, a seller who did not previously charge for a covered good or service may not charge a price that exceeds their cost by more than 50 percent.

The bill includes several exceptions. A price increase is allowed if it is directly attributable to additional supplier, labor, or materials costs and does not exceed the seller’s customary markup by more than 10 percent, or if it results from tariffs or national trade policies. Hotels and motels may raise rates due to regularly scheduled seasonal adjustments. Residential rent increases are permitted if they stem from amortized repair or improvement costs beyond normal maintenance or were contractually agreed to before the disaster.

The Federal Trade Commission enforces the prohibition, treating violations as unfair or deceptive acts under the FTC Act. State attorneys general may also bring civil actions for injunctions, damages, restitution, or other relief, with notice to the FTC. If the FTC or U.S. Attorney General files a federal action, parallel state actions against the same defendant are paused. Individuals who suffer actual monetary loss may sue for damages and injunctive relief, with the possibility of treble damages for willful violations and recovery of costs and attorney’s fees. Private suits must be brought within two years of discovering the violation.

Civil penalties may reach $25,000 per violation, but total liability for related acts is capped at $25,000. These amounts are adjusted annually for inflation. Recovered penalties are deposited into a fund to assist affected communities. The bill does not preempt state laws that do not conflict with it.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act
This bill prohibits certain price increases following presidentially declared major disasters or emergencies, requires the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) to enforce the prohibition, and authorizes states and private parties to bring legal action for violations.
The bill prohibits entities from increasing prices in affected areas by more than 10% above pre-disaster prices for 30 days after the declaration date for essential consumer goods and services (e.g., food, emergency supplies, and transportation), hotel lodging, and residential rental properties. For repair or reconstruction services this prohibition lasts 180 days. Also, for 30 days, entities may not charge more than 50% above their cost for such goods, services, or housing if they did not charge that price pre-disaster. The bill provides exceptions, including for higher costs.
Violations of the prohibition are subject to specified civil penalties. Amounts recovered by the FTC must be used to assist communities in areas affected by a major disaster or emergency.  
Additionally, the bill authorizes states, with prior notice to the FTC, to bring a civil action in state or federal court when their residents are threatened or affected by a violation of the bill. It also authorizes private parties to bring an action if commenced within two years after discovering the violation.

bills.title: Communities of Recovery Reauthorization Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Communities of Recovery Reauthorization Act of 2025 (H.R. 2695) amends the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize a federal grant program that supports building communities of recovery, which assist individuals recovering from substance use disorders.

The bill makes a single change to existing law. It revises Section 547(f) of the Public Health Service Act (42 U.S.C. 290ee-2(f)) by updating the program's funding authorization. Specifically, it removes the prior authorization of $5,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2019 through 2023 and replaces it with an authorization of $17,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

The bill does not alter the underlying purpose, eligibility requirements, or operation of the grant program itself; it only extends the period for which funds are authorized and increases the annual authorized amount. The legislation was introduced in the House of Representatives on April 7, 2025, and referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Introduced in the House of Representatives on April 7, 2025, by Representative Pettersen and co-sponsored by Representative Nunn of Iowa, H.R. 2695, titled the "Communities of Recovery Reauthorization Act of 2025," has been referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce. 

The primary purpose of the bill is to amend the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize federal grants that support building communities of recovery. To achieve this, the legislation proposes a specific modification to Section 547(f) of the Public Health Service Act, which is codified under Title 42 of the United States Code, Section 290ee-2(f). 

Under the existing statutory language, the grant program was authorized to receive appropriations of $5,000,000 for each fiscal year from 2019 through 2023. The bill would strike this previous funding provision and replace it with a new authorization of $17,000,000 for each fiscal year from 2026 through 2030. This change increases the annual authorized funding level for the grant program and establishes the new five-year period for these appropriations.

accurate.why: Accurately describes reauthorizing the Building Communities of Recovery grants ($5M->$17M, FY2026-2030) via PHSA sec. 547(f).

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Communities of Recovery Reauthorization Act of 2025 amends Section 547(f) of the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize and increase federal funding for grants that build communities of recovery. 

The bill's primary purpose is to extend the authorization of appropriations for this grant program and raise the annual funding cap. Under existing law, the program was authorized to receive $5,000,000 for each fiscal year from 2019 through 2023. This legislation strikes that provision and replaces it with an authorization of $17,000,000 for each fiscal year from 2026 through 2030. 

By making this change, the bill more than triples the authorized annual funding level and extends the program's authorization for an additional five-year period. The legislation consists of two sections: the first establishes the short title as the "Communities of Recovery Reauthorization Act of 2025," and the second details the amendment to the Public Health Service Act. The bill does not introduce any new material exceptions, conditions, or regulatory changes; it strictly modifies the authorized funding levels and the applicable fiscal years. The updated funding authorization applies to fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

bills.title: No Visas for Anti-Semitic Students Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "No Visas for Anti-Semitic Students Act" (H.R. 2866) directs the Secretary of State to revoke or deny student visas to foreign nationals who engage in antisemitic conduct.

Under Section 2, the Secretary of State must revoke or deny a visa issued under subparagraph (F) or (M) of section 101(a)(15) of the Immigration and Nationality Act—categories covering academic and vocational students—for any alien who, on or after the bill's enactment date, both (1) engages in "prohibited antisemitic conduct" and (2) is determined by the Secretary to pose potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States under section 237(a)(4)(C)(i) of the Act.

The bill defines "antisemitism" using the definition adopted by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance on May 26, 2016, as adopted by the State Department, including its "contemporary examples of antisemitism." It defines "prohibited antisemitic conduct" as either (A) committing a physical act of violence, vandalism, or harassment targeting a Jewish individual, their property, a community institution, or a religious facility, with intent to intimidate or harm based on Jewish identity or affiliation; or (B) providing material support—including funding, organizing, or inciting—to a person knowing they intended to use it to commit such an act.

Section 3 amends section 214 of the Immigration and Nationality Act by adding a new subsection (s). This provision states that an alien may not be granted or maintain (F) or (M) nonimmigrant status if the alien engages in prohibited antisemitic conduct and the Secretary determines such conduct would have potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences. It further requires the Secretary to revoke the visa of an alien already holding such status upon making these determinations. The new subsection incorporates the same definitions of "antisemitism" and "prohibited antisemitic conduct."

The bill's provisions apply to conduct occurring on or after the date of enactment. It was introduced on April 10, 2025, and referred to the Committee on the Judiciary.

The bill’s primary purpose is to require denial or revocation of certain student visas for noncitizens who engage in specified antisemitic conduct and are determined by the Secretary of State to pose potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States.

The bill applies to visas and nonimmigrant status under the F and M categories of the Immigration and Nationality Act, which cover academic students and vocational students. It directs the Secretary of State to revoke or deny such a visa to any noncitizen who, on or after the date of enactment, engages in “prohibited antisemitic conduct” and is determined, under existing immigration law, to pose potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences through that conduct.

The bill also amends section 214 of the Immigration and Nationality Act to provide that a noncitizen may not be granted or maintain F or M nonimmigrant status if the person engages in prohibited antisemitic conduct and the Secretary of State makes the required foreign-policy determination. If a person already granted F or M status is found to have engaged in such conduct and to meet that foreign-policy standard, the Secretary must revoke the person’s visa.

The bill defines “antisemitism” by reference to the definition adopted on May 26, 2016, by the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance, as adopted by the Department of State, including its contemporary examples. “Prohibited antisemitic conduct” means a physical act of violence, vandalism, or harassment targeting a Jewish individual, their property, a community institution, or religious facility, with intent to intimidate or harm based on Jewish identity or affiliation. It also includes providing material support, including funding, organizing, or inciting, to a person while knowing that the person intended to use the support for such an act.
