deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, titled the “No Visas for Anti-Semitic Students Act,” requires the Secretary of State to revoke or deny student visas (F or M nonimmigrant visas) for any foreign national who engages in certain antisemitic conduct and is determined to pose potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States.

Under the bill, the Secretary must act when two conditions are met. First, the individual must have committed “prohibited antisemitic conduct” on or after the date of enactment. That term covers two categories of behavior: (1) a physical act of violence, vandalism, or harassment targeting a Jewish person, their property, a community institution, or a religious facility, done with intent to intimidate or harm because of Jewish identity or affiliation; and (2) providing material support—such as funding, organizing, or inciting—to someone knowing that the recipient intended to use that support for such an act. Second, the Secretary must determine, using the authority under section 237(a)(4)(C)(i) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, that the individual or their conduct poses potentially serious adverse foreign policy consequences. The bill adopts the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance working definition of antisemitism, including its contemporary examples, as already used by the Department of State.

The bill also amends the Immigration and Nationality Act to add a new provision that bars an alien from being granted or maintaining F or M student status under the same conditions. If the Secretary determines that a current student visa holder has engaged in prohibited antisemitic conduct and poses the required foreign policy risk, the Secretary must revoke the visa.

The requirements apply to conduct occurring on or after the bill’s enactment. The bill does not create new exceptions; visa revocation or denial hinges on the dual factual findings regarding conduct and foreign policy consequences.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Treatment, Recovery, Education, Awareness, and Training for Youth Act" (or "TREAT Youth Act"), introduced as H.R. 3689 in the 119th Congress, amends the SUPPORT for Patients and Communities Act to reauthorize funding for an existing youth prevention and recovery initiative.

Specifically, the bill replaces the authorization-of-appropriations provision in Section 7102(c)(9) of that Act (codified at 42 U.S.C. 290bb-7a(c)(9)). It establishes new funding levels for the initiative across five fiscal years:

- $10,000,000 for fiscal year 2026
- $12,000,000 for fiscal year 2027
- $13,000,000 for fiscal year 2028
- $14,000,000 for fiscal year 2029
- $15,000,000 for fiscal year 2030

The bill does not alter the substance, eligibility, or operation of the underlying youth prevention and recovery program; it only updates the dollar amounts and years authorized for appropriation. The legislation was introduced on June 3, 2025, and referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce, with additional referral to the Committee on Education and Workforce.

bills.title: Safer Response Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Safer Response Act of 2025 (H.R. 4079) amends Section 546 of the Public Health Service Act to reauthorize and modify a federal grant program that supports the training of first responders and the provision of overdose-reversal drugs.

The bill makes several substantive changes to the existing program. First, it broadens the type of drugs covered by the program. Where current law refers to drugs "approved or cleared" by the Food and Drug Administration, the bill replaces this phrase throughout with "approved, cleared, or otherwise legally marketed," potentially expanding the categories of qualifying overdose-reversal drugs.

Second, the bill expands the program's scope beyond opioids. It removes the word "opioid" in multiple places, so that the grants and related training apply to drug overdoses generally rather than only opioid-related overdoses. Specifically, in the provision describing covered training, language referring to "opioid and heroin" overdoses is changed to "opioid, heroin, and other drug" overdoses, and references to "opioid overdose" are changed to "overdose."

Third, the bill reauthorizes funding for the program. It strikes the prior authorization of $36,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2019 through 2023 and replaces it with an authorization of $57,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

The bill also makes a technical change, capitalizing "Tribes and Tribal" where those terms appear in subsection (a).

The legislation was introduced in the House of Representatives on June 23, 2025, and referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce.

bills.title: SECURE Grid Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Securing Community Upgrades for a Resilient Grid Act" or "SECURE Grid Act" (H.R. 7257) amends Section 366 of the Energy Policy and Conservation Act to require States to address the physical security, cybersecurity, and resilience of local electricity distribution systems within their State energy security plans.

The bill defines a "local distribution system" as energy infrastructure owned and operated by an electric utility at 100 kilovolts or less. It expands the consultation requirement so that States coordinate not only with owners and operators of energy infrastructure but also with suppliers of equipment for generating, transmitting, and distributing electricity.

The bill revises the content requirements for State plans. Plans must address physical threats and vulnerabilities (including weather-related threats, physical attacks on local distribution and bulk-power systems, and supply-chain risks for electricity equipment) and cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities (including threats to local distribution systems that may affect the bulk-power system). Plans must also provide a risk-mitigation approach for responding to, mitigating, and recovering from such hazards.

The legislation clarifies that, while a State must submit a plan to be eligible for certain assistance, the submission does not require Secretary of Energy approval. It changes the provision governing federal technical assistance from discretionary ("may") to mandatory ("shall"). It also extends information-protection provisions to cover local distribution systems and sets a new sunset date of September 30, 2031, for Section 366.

Section 3 directs the Comptroller General (GAO) to submit a report by September 30, 2030, to the House Energy and Commerce Committee and the Senate Energy and Natural Resources Committee. The report must evaluate the effectiveness of State energy security plans, offer recommendations for improving State capabilities and federal coordination, and provide information on federal financial assistance used to implement plans and related State activities. The report must be publicly available, except that information protected under Section 366(h) must be placed in a non-public annex.

As reported by the Committee on Energy and Commerce, the text was amended by striking all language after the enacting clause and inserting new text.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The SECURE Grid Act amends the Energy Policy and Conservation Act requirements for State energy security plans to include greater attention to the security and resilience of local electric distribution systems and related supply chains.

The bill defines a “local distribution system” as energy infrastructure owned and operated by an electric utility at a voltage of 100 kilovolts or less. It requires State energy security plans to address potential hazards to each energy sector or system, including weather-related physical threats, physical attacks on local distribution systems and the bulk-power system, supply chain risks for equipment used to generate, transmit, and distribute electricity, and cybersecurity threats and vulnerabilities, including those affecting local distribution systems that may impact the bulk-power system. It also revises the required risk mitigation approach in State plans to include methods for responding to, mitigating, and recovering from those hazards.

The bill expands coordination and plan-related considerations to include suppliers of equipment for the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity, in addition to owners and operators of energy infrastructure. It provides that a State’s required submission of an energy security plan or update does not need approval by the Secretary of Energy. It changes the Secretary’s authority to provide assistance to States under this section from discretionary to mandatory. It also extends protections for certain information from disclosure to include information related to local distribution systems. The section governing State energy security plans would expire on September 30, 2031.

The bill requires the Comptroller General, by September 30, 2030, to report to specified House and Senate committees on the effectiveness of State energy security plans, including their role in risk identification, mitigation, response, and recovery; federal assistance used for implementation; State activities; implementation of the amended law; and recommendations for improving State and Department of Energy coordination. Protected information must be placed in a nonpublic annex.

The "Securing Community Upgrades for a Resilient Grid Act" or the "SECURE Grid Act" (H.R. 7257) amends the Energy Policy and Conservation Act to require states to address the physical security, cybersecurity, and resilience of local distribution systems in their State energy security plans. The bill defines a "local distribution system" as energy infrastructure owned and operated by an electric utility at a voltage of 100 kilovolts or less. 

Under the bill, state energy security plans must address physical threats—including weather-related vulnerabilities, physical attacks on local distribution and bulk-power systems, and supply chain risks for electricity generation, transmission, and distribution equipment. The plans must also address cybersecurity threats to local distribution systems that could impact the bulk-power system, and they must provide risk mitigation approaches to enhance reliability and end-use resilience. Additionally, the bill expands coordination and planning requirements to include suppliers of electricity generation, transmission, and distribution equipment. 

The legislation clarifies that while states must submit these energy security plans to secure eligibility for certain federal programs, the submissions do not require approval by the Secretary of Energy. It also changes certain federal assistance provisions from optional to mandatory and ensures that protected information regarding local distribution systems is excluded from public disclosure. 

Finally, the bill establishes two key deadlines. First, the Comptroller General must submit a report to Congress by September 30, 2030, evaluating the efficacy of these state energy security plans, including how they have improved risk mitigation and how federal financial assistance was used. Second, the amended state energy security plan provisions are set to expire on September 30, 2031.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Securing Community Upgrades for a Resilient Grid Act or the SECURE Grid Act
This bill extends through FY2031 and modifies requirements for state energy security plans. Under current law, states are required to submit to the Department of Energy (DOE) state energy security plans that meet certain requirements in order to be eligible to receive federal financial assistance for state energy conservation programs. 
The bill specifies that DOE is not required to approve the plans. 
The bill requires DOE to provide information, technical assistance, and other assistance in the development, implementation, or revision of a state energy security plan upon the request of a state.
The bill also requires states to address additional factors in their energy security plans, such as (1) weather-related threats and vulnerabilities; (2) supply chain risks for equipment for the generation, transmission, and distribution of electricity; and (3) the security of local distribution systems and the bulk-power system. A local distribution system is energy infrastructure owned and operated by an electric utility at a voltage of 100 kilovolts or less.
In addition, the bill directs the Government Accountability Office to report to Congress on the efficacy of state energy security plans.

bills.title: Energy Emergency Leadership Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Energy Emergency Leadership Act (H.R. 7258) amends the Department of Energy Organization Act to formally assign energy emergency and energy security functions to an Assistant Secretary within the Department of Energy.

Specifically, the bill adds a new paragraph to Section 203(a) of the Act, designating responsibilities related to energy emergencies and security. These functions include responsibilities concerning energy infrastructure, security and resilience, emerging threats, cybersecurity, supply, and emergency planning, preparedness, coordination, response, and restoration. The provision also authorizes the Department, upon request from a State, local, or Tribal government or an energy sector entity, to provide technical assistance and support to protect against, detect, and respond to energy security threats, risks, and incidents. Such assistance is to be carried out in coordination with other federal agencies as appropriate.

The bill further directs the Secretary of Energy to ensure that these newly assigned functions are performed in coordination with relevant federal agencies.

The legislation does not specify an effective date or set deadlines, and it does not establish new funding. The bill was introduced in the House on January 27, 2026, referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and subsequently ordered to be printed after being committed to the Committee of the Whole House.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Energy Emergency Leadership Act (H.R. 7258) amends the Department of Energy Organization Act to explicitly assign energy emergency and energy security functions to the Department of Energy’s Assistant Secretaries. 

Under the bill, these newly assigned responsibilities include addressing energy infrastructure, security and resilience, emerging threats, cybersecurity, and energy supply. The Assistant Secretaries will also oversee emergency planning, preparedness, coordination, response, and restoration efforts. 

Additionally, the bill authorizes the provision of technical assistance and support to protect against, detect, and respond to energy security threats, risks, and incidents. This assistance is provided upon the request of a State, local, or Tribal government, or an energy sector entity, and must be conducted in coordination with other Federal agencies as appropriate. Finally, the bill directs the Secretary of Energy to ensure that all of these energy emergency and security functions are performed in coordination with relevant Federal agencies.

accurate.why: Accurately describes adding energy emergency/security functions for DOE Assistant Secretaries.

The Energy Emergency Leadership Act amends the Department of Energy Organization Act to formally assign energy emergency and energy security functions to an Assistant Secretary of Energy. Specifically, the bill adds a new paragraph to Section 203(a) of the Act, detailing the responsibilities associated with these functions.

Under the new provision, the assigned Assistant Secretary would be responsible for matters concerning energy infrastructure, security and resilience, emerging threats, cybersecurity, supply, and emergency planning, preparedness, coordination, response, and restoration. Additionally, upon the request of a State, local, or Tribal government or an energy sector entity, the Assistant Secretary would provide technical assistance and support to protect against, detect, and respond to energy security threats, risks, and incidents. This assistance must be provided in coordination with other Federal agencies as appropriate.

The bill also mandates that the Secretary of Energy ensure these newly assigned functions are performed in coordination with relevant Federal agencies. The legislation does not specify a separate effective date or deadline, meaning its provisions would take effect upon enactment.

bills.title: Rural and Municipal Utility Cybersecurity Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Rural and Municipal Utility Cybersecurity Act" (H.R. 7266) amends Section 40124 of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to reauthorize and revise the Rural and Municipal Utility Advanced Cybersecurity Grant and Technical Assistance Program, which is administered by the Secretary (of Energy).

The bill directs the Secretary to maintain this program, which provides technical assistance and funding—including grants, cooperative agreements, and prizes—to eligible electric utilities to help them protect against, detect, respond to, and recover from cybersecurity threats. The program's stated objectives are to deploy advanced cybersecurity technologies for electric utility systems and to increase participation of eligible entities in cybersecurity threat information-sharing programs.

The bill defines "eligible entity" to include rural electric cooperatives; electric utilities owned by political subdivisions of a State (such as municipally owned utilities); utilities owned by agencies, authorities, corporations, or instrumentalities of one or more political subdivisions; certain not-for-profit entities partnered with at least six such utilities; and investor-owned utilities selling less than 4,000,000 megawatt hours of electricity per year. It also defines terms such as "advanced cybersecurity technology," "bulk-power system," "cybersecurity threat," and "defense critical electric infrastructure" by reference to existing law.

In carrying out the program, the Secretary must (subject to available appropriations) provide assistance and funding on a competitive or noncompetitive basis, develop award criteria, and establish a process to inform eligible entities of opportunities. The Secretary may enter into agreements with eligible entities. Priority is to be given to entities that have limited cybersecurity resources, own assets critical to bulk-power system reliability, or own or operate defense critical electric infrastructure.

Information shared by or with federal, State, Tribal, or local governments under the program is deemed voluntarily shared and exempt from disclosure under the federal Freedom of Information Act and comparable State, Tribal, or local open-records laws.

The bill authorizes $250,000,000 in appropriations to the Secretary for fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Rural and Municipal Utility Cybersecurity Act (H.R. 7266) amends the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to reauthorize and modify the Rural and Municipal Utility Advanced Cybersecurity Grant and Technical Assistance Program. The bill's primary purpose is to maintain a federal program that provides technical assistance and funding—including grants, cooperative agreements, and prizes—to help eligible electric utilities protect against, detect, respond to, and recover from cybersecurity threats. 

Eligible entities under the program include rural electric cooperatives, municipally owned electric utilities, other utilities owned by political subdivisions, certain not-for-profit partnerships, and investor-owned electric utilities that sell less than 4,000,000 megawatt hours of electricity per year. The program's core objectives are to deploy advanced cybersecurity technologies and increase utility participation in threat information sharing programs.

In administering the program, the Secretary must establish a process to inform eligible entities of available opportunities. The bill directs the Secretary to prioritize entities that have limited cybersecurity resources, own assets critical to the bulk-power system's reliability, or own or operate defense critical electric infrastructure. 

Additionally, the bill mandates that any information shared with or by federal, state, tribal, or local governments under the program is considered voluntarily shared. Consequently, this information is exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA) and any equivalent state, tribal, or local open records, open meetings, or sunshine laws. 

To fund these initiatives, the bill authorizes $250,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

The bill replaces Section 40124 of the existing law, defining "eligible entities" as rural electric cooperatives, municipally owned electric utilities, utilities owned by state political subdivisions, specific not-for-profit partnerships involving at least six such utilities, and investor-owned utilities selling fewer than 4,000,000 megawatt hours of electricity annually. Under the program, the Secretary of Energy is directed to provide technical assistance and award grants, cooperative agreements, and prizes on a competitive or noncompetitive basis, subject to available appropriations. The program's objectives are to deploy advanced cybersecurity technologies for electric utility systems and increase participation in cybersecurity threat information sharing programs.

When awarding funding and assistance, the Secretary must give priority to eligible entities that have limited cybersecurity resources, own assets critical to the reliability of the bulk-power system, or own or operate defense critical electric infrastructure. The Secretary is also required to develop award criteria and establish a process to ensure eligible entities are informed about funding opportunities.

Additionally, the bill stipulates that information shared by or with federal, state, tribal, or local governments under the program is deemed voluntarily shared and is exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act and similar state, tribal, or local laws. The bill authorizes $250,000,000 in appropriations to carry out the program for fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

Eligible entities now include rural electric cooperatives; municipally owned electric utilities; electric utilities owned by state political subdivisions or related agencies; not-for-profit organizations partnering with at least six of the above types of utilities; and investor-owned electric utilities that sell less than 4,000,000 megawatt hours of electricity per year.

Under the program, the Secretary of Energy is directed to provide technical assistance and award funding through grants, cooperative agreements, and prizes. These awards may be made on a competitive or noncompetitive basis. The Secretary must develop criteria for awarding assistance and establish a process to ensure all eligible entities are informed about available opportunities. The program’s objectives are to deploy advanced cybersecurity technologies for electric utility systems and to increase eligible entities’ participation in cybersecurity threat information sharing programs.

When providing assistance, the Secretary must give priority to eligible entities that have limited cybersecurity resources, own assets critical to the reliability of the bulk-power system, or own or operate defense critical electric infrastructure.

The bill also provides that information shared under the program is considered voluntarily shared and is exempt from disclosure under the federal Freedom of Information Act and similar state, tribal, or local open records laws. It authorizes $250,000,000 in appropriations for fiscal years 2026 through 2030 to carry out the program.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Rural and Municipal Utility Cybersecurity Act
This bill reauthorizes through FY2030 a program that provides cybersecurity-related assistance and funding to rural electric cooperatives and municipal or small, investor-owned electric utilities. The bill also revises methods of awarding support and expands protections for information shared under the program.
This program, known as the Rural and Municipal Advanced Cybersecurity Grant and Technical Assistance Program, is administered by the Department of Energy (DOE). Current law requires DOE to provide technical assistance and funding under the program on a competitive basis; under the bill, assistance and funding may be provided on a competitive or noncompetitive basis.
Current law also requires DOE to prioritize providing technical assistance and funding to certain entities, including entities that own defense critical electric infrastructure. The bill specifies that DOE must also prioritize entities that operate such infrastructure. (Defense critical electric infrastructure is electric infrastructure that (1) is located in the contiguous United States, and (2) serves a designated critical defense facility but is not owned or operated by the owner or operator of the critical facility.)
Finally, the bill expands an existing protection against disclosure to cover all information shared under the program by or with the federal government or a state, tribal, or local government. This provision protects against disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act and other state, tribal, or local disclosure laws.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes prioritization and disclosure-protection conditions.

effective_dates.why: FY2030 correctly noted.

bills.title: Energy Threat Analysis Center Act of 2026

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Energy Threat Analysis Center Act of 2026" (H.R. 7305) amends Section 40125(c) of the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to reauthorize and expand the Department of Energy's Energy Sector Operational Support for Cyberresilience Program, which provides operational support for cybersecurity and resilience in the U.S. energy sector.

The bill adds new purposes to the program aimed at strengthening the collective defense, response, and resilience of the energy sector. These include enhancing collaboration between the government and the energy sector to analyze and mitigate threats by exchanging classified and unclassified information, building technical infrastructure for advanced analytics and threat analysis, advancing understanding of national security risks and vulnerabilities that adversaries might exploit, and increasing knowledge of threat actor tactics, techniques, and indicators of compromise.

The bill authorizes the Secretary of Energy to carry out program activities through an Energy Threat Analysis Center, which may be established at one or more physical locations.

Several procedural provisions are added:
- The provision of assistance or information to any government or private entity is at the Secretary's sole and unreviewable discretion and creates no right or benefit (substantive or procedural) for other entities to receive similar assistance.
- The program is not subject to the Federal Advisory Committee Act (chapter 10 of title 5).
- Information shared with or by federal, state, tribal, or local governments under the program is deemed voluntarily shared and exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act and comparable state, tribal, or local disclosure laws, and must be withheld from the public.
- The Secretary is granted transaction authority to enter into contracts, cooperative agreements, grants, and other transactions with public agencies, private organizations, and persons, and may establish pre-approved national security contracting mechanisms, model partnership agreements, and expedited review procedures to minimize delays.

Finally, the bill updates the program's authorization of appropriations period from fiscal years 2022 through 2026 to fiscal years 2027 through 2031.

The bill was introduced February 2, 2026, and reported with an amendment on May 12, 2026.

The bill amends the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to reauthorize and revise the Department of Energy’s Energy Sector Operational Support for Cyberresilience Program, which supports cybersecurity and resilience in the energy sector. It changes the program’s authorization period from fiscal years 2022 through 2026 to fiscal years 2027 through 2031.

The bill adds a new stated purpose for the program: strengthening the collective defense, response, and resilience of the U.S. energy sector. It directs this purpose toward greater collaboration between government and the energy sector to analyze threats; deny, disrupt, and mitigate operational impacts to energy systems; exchange classified and unclassified information; jointly analyze potential and realized threats; provide recommendations to mitigate threats; and build technical infrastructure for analytics, experimentation, alerts, discovery, and sharing actionable insights. It also includes improving understanding of national security risks and vulnerabilities in the energy sector and of threat actor tactics, techniques, procedures, indicators of compromise, capabilities, and activities. The bill also removes one previously listed program purpose after redesignating the existing purpose provisions.

The bill authorizes the Secretary of Energy to carry out program activities through an Energy Threat Analysis Center, which may be established at one or more physical locations. It provides that assistance or information under the program is provided solely at the Secretary’s unreviewable discretion and does not create any right or entitlement for other entities to similar assistance or information.

The bill specifies that the program is not an advisory committee under federal advisory committee law. It exempts information shared by or with federal, state, Tribal, or local governments under the program from public disclosure under the federal Freedom of Information Act and comparable state, Tribal, or local disclosure laws. It also authorizes the Secretary to use contracts, cooperative agreements, grants, and other transactions with public or private entities to carry out the program, including expedited and pre-approved national security contracting and partnership mechanisms.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, titled the Energy Threat Analysis Center Act of 2026, amends the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act to reauthorize and expand the Department of Energy’s Energy Sector Operational Support for Cyberresilience Program. Its primary purpose is to strengthen the collective defense, response, and resilience of the U.S. energy sector against cyber and other threats.

The legislation adds new objectives to the program, including enhancing government-industry collaboration to analyze threats, exchange classified and unclassified information, and provide actionable mitigation recommendations. It also aims to advance understanding of national security risks and adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures. To carry out these activities, the Secretary of Energy is authorized to establish an Energy Threat Analysis Center at one or more physical locations.

Several provisions govern how the program operates. The Secretary has sole and unreviewable discretion to provide assistance or information, and such provision does not create any right or benefit for other entities to receive similar support. The program is explicitly exempt from the Federal Advisory Committee Act. Information shared by or with federal, state, tribal, or local governments under the program is deemed voluntarily shared and is exempt from disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act and any corresponding state, tribal, or local laws; it must be withheld from the public without discretion.

The bill grants the Secretary broad transaction authority, allowing contracts, cooperative agreements, grants, and other transactions with public agencies, private organizations, and individuals. To minimize delays, the Secretary may use pre-approved national security contracting mechanisms, model partnership agreements, and expedited review procedures. Finally, the measure extends the program’s funding authorization from fiscal years 2022 through 2026 to fiscal years 2027 through 2031.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Energy Threat Analysis Center Act of 2026
This bill reauthorizes through FY2031 and expands the pilot Energy Sector Operational Support for Cyber Resilience Program of the Department of Energy (DOE). The program aims to protect energy infrastructure through collaboration between the federal government and the energy sector and enhancing DOE’s emergency response capabilities.
Specifically, the bill expands the program objectives to include 
enhancing collaboration between the government and the energy sector to address threats to energy systems,
advancing the collective understanding of national security risks and vulnerabilities associated with the energy sector that may be exploited by adversaries, and
helping the energy sector increase its understanding of tactics of adversaries that present risks to the energy sector.
The bill allows DOE to establish an Energy Threat Analysis Center at one or more physical locations for program activities.
The bill also eliminates technical assistance provided under the program to small electric utilities.
Under the bill, the decision to provide assistance or information under the program to a governmental or private entity is at the sole discretion of the Secretary of Energy and is unreviewable.
The bill authorizes the Secretary to (1) enter into and perform contracts, grants, and other transactions with public agencies, private organizations, and persons to carry out the program; and (2) establish and utilize preapproved national security contracting mechanisms, model partnership agreements, and expedited review procedures for purposes of entering into such transactions.
In addition, the bill exempts the program from certain public disclosure and other transparency requirements.

bills.title: KIDS Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) is a comprehensive bill aimed at protecting minors (defined as individuals under 17) online. It establishes new requirements across several categories of online services and provides for enforcement by the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general.

**Title I (SCREEN Act):** Within one year of enactment, websites where more than one-third of content is "sexual material harmful to minors" must adopt age-verification technology to prevent minors' access. The bill prohibits requiring users to submit government-issued ID and requires data-security protections.

**Title II:** This title applies to social media-type "covered platforms." Subtitle A (Kids Online Safety Act) requires platforms to adopt policies addressing harms to minors (e.g., sexual exploitation, sale of drugs/alcohol/gambling, financial deception); provide safeguards and parental tools (with default protective settings); offer reporting mechanisms with deadlines (10 days, or sooner for imminent threats); disclose advertising practices; and undergo annual third-party audits. Subtitle B (SMK Act) bans ephemeral messaging for minors and direct messaging for children under 13, and requires parental controls for teens' messaging. Subtitle C (SPY Kids Act) restricts market research on minors.

**Title III (Safer GAMING Act):** Online video game providers must give parents communication-limiting safeguards (enabled by default at the most protective level) and other controls.

**Title IV (SAFE BOTs Act):** AI chatbot providers must disclose that the chatbot is not human, provide crisis-hotline resources, suggest breaks after three hours, and avoid falsely claiming to be licensed professionals.

**Title V:** Requires multiple studies and reports (on social media use, fentanyl access, safety tools, and chatbots' mental-health effects), educational campaigns, and a Kids Internet Safety Partnership.

**Title VI:** Provides for FTC and state enforcement, channels constitutional challenges exclusively to the D.C. Circuit (with 90/60-day limits), and includes rules preserving encryption, Section 230, and COPPA.

Most provisions take effect one year after enactment, with exceptions (e.g., Subtitle B at 180 days, Subtitle C at 90 days). The bill preempts certain conflicting state laws while preserving others.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act would establish federal requirements intended to protect minors online, with enforcement by the Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general.

The bill would require certain publicly accessible online platforms where more than one-third of content is sexual material harmful to minors to use commercially available age-verification or age-estimation technology, beginning one year after enactment, and to prevent minors from accessing that material. Self-attestation would not be sufficient, government identification could not be required, and verification data could not be retained or shared beyond what is strictly necessary.

For covered social-media-like platforms, the bill would require policies addressing specified harms to minors, including severe threats of violence, sexual exploitation and abuse, illegal or age-restricted products, and financial harms from deceptive practices. Platforms would have to provide default protective safeguards for known minors, parental tools, reporting mechanisms with timely responses, notices, advertising disclosures, and annual independent audits, with the first audit due within 18 months. The bill states these provisions do not require age gating or age verification.

The bill would prohibit covered platforms from offering ephemeral messaging to known minors and direct messaging to known users under 13, and would require parental direct-message controls for known teen users. These provisions generally take effect 180 days after enactment, except the teen parental-control section, which is subject to the Act’s general one-year effective date. The bill also prohibits certain market or product-focused research on known minors, with exceptions for safety-related purposes and legal compliance; that provision takes effect after 90 days.

The bill would require online video game providers to give parents default protective controls over communications, purchases, time limits, and profile recommendations for known minor users. Chatbot providers could not falsely state that a chatbot is a licensed professional, would have to disclose to known minors that the chatbot is AI, provide crisis hotline resources when suicide is raised, prompt breaks after three hours, and maintain policies addressing specified harmful content.

The bill also directs federal studies, reports, educational resources, and a temporary Kids Internet Safety Partnership, and amends the Protecting Children in the 21st Century Act to create an FTC-led internet safety education campaign. It includes state-law preemption provisions with exceptions, encryption protections, severability, and special rules for constitutional challenges.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) aims to protect minors under age 17 from online harms, restrict their access to harmful content, and provide parents with tools to manage their children's digital interactions. 

To achieve this, the bill establishes several new requirements for online platforms. Platforms where more than one-third of the content is sexually explicit must use age-verification technologies to block minors. The bill also bans ephemeral messaging for all minors, prohibits direct messaging for children under 13, and requires parental consent and controls for teen direct messaging. Additionally, covered platforms and online video game providers must offer high-protection default privacy settings, parental monitoring tools, and options to restrict purchases, track screen time, and opt out of personalized recommendation algorithms. Platforms must also undergo annual independent safety audits.

Furthermore, the bill prohibits market research on minors, with exceptions for research used solely to improve platform safety or comply with laws. AI chatbot providers must disclose that they are not human, provide crisis hotline resources when prompted about suicide, and advise users to take a break after three hours of continuous use. The bill also amends the Protecting Children in the 21st Century Act to mandate a public online safety campaign, establishes the Kids Internet Safety Partnership, and orders federal studies on the impacts of social media, fentanyl access, and chatbots on minors.

The Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general will enforce the Act. Most provisions take effect one year after enactment, though the market research ban takes effect in 90 days and messaging restrictions in 180 days. Any constitutional challenges to the Act must be filed in the D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals within 90 days of enactment.

accurate.why: Accurate, well-organized summary of all major KIDS Act duties, thresholds, effective dates, and the D.C. Circuit review provision.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) aims to protect minors online and empower parents. The bill establishes requirements for online platforms, social media, video games, and AI chatbots.

Under Title I, platforms where over one-third of content is sexual material harmful to minors must use technology verification measures to prevent minors from accessing such content, effective one year after enactment. This preempts conflicting state laws.

Title II applies to social media-like platforms. It requires providers to implement policies addressing specific harms to minors, such as sexual exploitation, drug distribution, and severe threats of violence. Platforms must provide safeguards for minors—like limiting communication, restricting geolocation, and controlling personalized recommendations—defaulting to the most protective settings. Providers must offer parental tools to manage privacy, purchases, and screen time, and are subject to annual independent audits. The bill prohibits ephemeral messaging for minors and direct messaging for children under 13, while requiring parental controls for teen direct messaging. It also bans market research on minors unless used for safety or legal compliance.

Title III requires online video game providers to offer safeguards for minors, such as limiting communication and purchases, defaulting to the most restrictive settings.

Title IV regulates AI chatbots, prohibiting them from falsely claiming to be licensed professionals. It requires clear disclosure that the chatbot is an AI system and mandates providing crisis resources when prompted. Providers must also implement policies to advise users to take breaks after three hours of continuous use.

Title V directs federal agencies to conduct studies and reports on social media use, fentanyl access, and chatbot impacts on mental health. It also establishes a public awareness campaign and the Kids Internet Safety Partnership to identify best practices.

The Act is enforced by the Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general. Most provisions take effect one year after enactment, though specific subtitles take effect after 90 or 180 days. The bill includes rules of construction stating it does not weaken encryption, alter Section 230 of the Communications Act, or require indefinite data retention.

accurate.why: Accurate, well-organized summary of all five titles of the KIDS Act including effective dates and rules of construction.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act (KIDS Act) establishes a federal framework to protect minors online by imposing new requirements on certain websites, apps, online video games, and artificial intelligence chatbots. The bill defines a minor as an individual under 17 years old.

Title I requires online platforms where more than one-third of the material is sexual content harmful to minors to use commercially available age-verification technology to block minors from accessing that material. It prohibits the retention or transfer of verification data beyond what is strictly necessary and does not require government-issued identification. This title preempts state laws on the same subject.

Title II applies to platforms that enable user-generated content sharing, use design features to promote engagement, and use personal information for advertising or recommendations. It requires these platforms to implement policies addressing harms such as threats of violence, sexual exploitation, and promotion of narcotics or gambling to minors. Platforms must provide minors with safeguards to limit communications, control recommendation systems, restrict geolocation sharing, and reduce compulsive usage features, with the most protective settings enabled by default. Parental tools must allow parents to manage privacy settings, restrict purchases, and view time spent. Platforms must offer reporting mechanisms, disclose advertising labels, and prohibit advertising illegal products to known minors. Annual third-party audits are required, with public reporting of certain metrics. This title also bans ephemeral messaging features for all minors and direct messaging for children under 13, while requiring parental controls for direct messaging for teens. It prohibits market or product-focused research on minors except for safety, security, or legal compliance purposes. State laws conflicting with these provisions are preempted.

Title III requires online video game providers to offer parents safeguards to limit communications, restrict purchases, and manage screen time for minor users, with the most protective settings as the default.

Title IV requires chatbot providers to disclose that a chatbot is an artificial intelligence system, provide crisis hotline resources when prompted about suicide, and implement policies to address access to harmful material and promote breaks after three hours of continuous use. Chatbots are prohibited from falsely claiming to be licensed professionals.

Title V directs the Federal Trade Commission and other agencies to conduct studies on social media use, fentanyl access, and chatbot mental health effects, and to develop public education resources. It also establishes a Kids Internet Safety Partnership to identify best practices.

The bill generally takes effect one year after enactment, with specific provisions taking effect earlier. Enforcement is carried out by the Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general, with violations treated as unfair or deceptive acts. The bill includes rules of construction stating it does not require weakening encryption, collecting additional age data, or disclosing private communications.

crs_reference.summary:
Kids Internet and Digital Safety Act or the KIDS Act
This bill requires specified online platforms to establish safeguards for minors. The safeguards include (1) limiting access to specified sexual material, (2) providing parental controls on social media and online video game platforms, and (3) requiring artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots to disclose certain information to users who are minors.
First, publicly available online platforms on which more than one-third of the content is considered sexual material harmful to minors under the bill must adopt technology to identify minors and prevent them from accessing such material.
Next, social media platforms must (1) implement default settings for minors that limit compulsive usage features and the ability of other users to communicate with minors, and (2) provide tools for parents to manage the privacy and account settings of a minor. Social media platforms may not allow ephemeral messaging features for minors.
The bill also requires online video game platforms to provide tools that allow parents to (1) limit communication between a minor and other users of the platform, and (2) restrict purchases by a minor on the platform. 
Further, providers of AI chatbots must disclose to users who are minors (1) that the chatbot is an AI system and not a human, and (2) suicide and crisis intervention hotline information.
Finally, the bill requires specified studies and reports about the effects of social media platforms on minors and provides for enforcement of the bill's requirements by the Federal Trade Commission and state attorneys general.

bills.title: Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act" (H.R. 8884) amends Title II of the Social Security Act to reauthorize the Social Security Administration's authority to conduct experiments and demonstration projects related to the disability insurance program. This demonstration authority had previously lapsed (set to expire in 2021–2022 under prior law).

The bill makes several specific changes:

- **Reauthorization timeline:** It extends the authority to initiate demonstration projects through December 31, 2030, and sets the authority to carry out such projects to terminate on December 31, 2031.

- **Waiver authority:** It extends through December 31, 2030, the Commissioner's authority to waive compliance with certain benefit requirements when conducting projects. It also lengthens the advance notice period to Congress from 90 days to 120 days, and requires that the report describing a project include the evaluation metrics to be used.

- **Funding source:** It revises how costs are paid. Administrative expenditures for these projects are to come from funds available for administering Title II. Benefits payable to individuals participating in the projects are to be paid from either the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund or the Federal Disability Insurance Trust Fund, as the Commissioner determines appropriate.

- **Participant protection:** It adds a new requirement that any project must ensure that a participant's total income will not be reduced because of their participation.

- **Technical amendments:** It corrects a reference in the statute and strikes subsection (f) of Section 234.

The amendments made by the bill take effect on January 1, 2027.

The bill was introduced May 19, 2026, by Mr. Austin Scott of Georgia, referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and reported with an amendment on June 25, 2026.

It also extends, through December 31, 2030, the Commissioner of Social Security’s authority to waive certain benefit requirements when conducting these experiments or demonstration projects.

For projects requiring such waivers, the bill changes the advance notice period to Congress from 90 days to 120 days. It also requires the notice to include the evaluation metrics that will be used for the experiment or demonstration project, in addition to expected annual and total costs.

The bill revises how expenses are paid. Administrative expenditures for these projects must come from funds available for administering title II. Benefits paid to or on behalf of participants because of their participation must come from either the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund or the Federal Disability Insurance Trust Fund, as determined appropriate by the Commissioner.

The bill adds a limitation that an individual’s total income may not be reduced because of participation in an experiment or demonstration project. It also makes technical amendments, including removing an obsolete subsection.

The amendments take effect on January 1, 2027.

The "Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act" (H.R. 8884) temporarily reauthorizes the Social Security Administration's authority to conduct experiments and demonstration projects under the disability insurance program. 

Specifically, the bill extends the deadline to initiate these projects to December 31, 2030, and extends the termination date for carrying them out to December 31, 2031. It also extends the authority to waive compliance with certain benefit requirements to December 31, 2030, increases a related statutory timeline from 90 days to 120 days, and requires the inclusion of specific evaluation metrics for each project. 

Under the bill, administrative expenditures for these experiments and demonstration projects must be paid from funds made available for administering Title II of the Social Security Act. Benefits payable to participants must be paid from either the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund or the Federal Disability Insurance Trust Fund, as determined appropriate by the Commissioner of Social Security. 

Additionally, the bill introduces a protective limitation, ensuring that an individual's total income will not be reduced as a result of participating in an experiment or demonstration project. z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The bill, titled the "Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act," amends title II of the Social Security Act to temporarily reauthorize and modify the demonstration project authority for the disability insurance program.

Under the bill, the authority to conduct experiments and demonstration projects under Section 234 of the Social Security Act, which had previously expired, is extended. The deadline to initiate projects is moved to December 31, 2030, and the authority to carry out such projects will terminate on December 31, 2031. The bill also extends the waiver authority for compliance with benefit requirements to December 31, 2030, and increases a specified timeframe from 90 to 120 days. Additionally, it requires the inclusion of evaluation metrics for experiments or demonstration projects.

The legislation amends Section 201(k) to clarify that administrative expenditures for these projects will be paid from funds available for the administration of the title, while benefits paid to participants will be drawn from either the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund or the Federal Disability Insurance Trust Fund, as determined appropriate by the Commissioner of Social Security.

The bill adds a new condition to the demonstration projects, stipulating that the total income of an individual will not be reduced due to their participation in an experiment or demonstration project. It also makes technical amendments, including replacing the word "subsection" with "section" in Section 234(d)(1) and striking subsection (f) of Section 234. The amendments made by the bill will take effect on January 1, 2027.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The bill extends and modifies the Social Security Administration’s authority to conduct demonstration projects under the disability insurance program. Its primary purpose is to temporarily reauthorize experiments and demonstration projects that test changes to program rules, with the aim of removing barriers to work for disabled Americans.

The legislation amends Section 234 of the Social Security Act. It pushes back the sunset of demonstration authority: projects may be initiated through December 31, 2030, and the authority to carry them out ends on December 31, 2031. Under previous law, those dates were December 31, 2021, and December 31, 2022, respectively. The Commissioner’s ability to waive compliance with certain benefit requirements in connection with these projects is also extended from December 30, 2021, to December 31, 2030.

Several procedural and substantive changes apply. The period for reporting to Congress on a proposed experiment or demonstration is expanded from 90 days to 120 days. When requesting a waiver, the Commissioner must now include evaluation metrics along with estimated annual and total costs. The bill also adjusts how project costs are paid: administrative expenditures are to come from funds provided for administering the title, while benefits paid to participants will come from the Federal Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund or the Federal Disability Insurance Trust Fund, as the Commissioner determines appropriate. A new safeguard is added, requiring that no approved experiment or demonstration project reduces a participant’s total income. Finally, the bill makes minor technical corrections and deletes a subsection that is being repealed.

The amendments take effect on January 1, 2027.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Removing Barriers to Work for Disabled Americans Act
This bill reauthorizes through 2031 the Social Security Administration’s (SSA’s) authority to carry out demonstration projects within the Social Security Disability Insurance (SSDI) program. The bill also imposes requirements related to beneficiary income, project funding, and SSA reporting.
SSDI demonstration projects must be designed to promote attachment to the workforce and generally involve temporary changes to SSDI policies tested on a subset of beneficiaries. Participation in a demonstration project must be voluntary. SSA’s authority to carry out these projects expired on December 31, 2022.
Under the bill, SSA must ensure that an individual’s participation in a demonstration project does not reduce their total income. Further, the bill specifies that administrative expenses related to demonstration projects must be paid from funds appropriated for administration.
Finally, the bill requires SSA to report to Congress 120 days before initiating a demonstration project. This is an increase from 90 days under current law. This advance notification must include evaluation metrics for the relevant project.

exceptions_conditions.why: Notes voluntary participation and the no-income-reduction condition.

effective_dates.why: Notes 2022 expiration and reauthorization through 2031.

major_provisions_covered.why: Covers the 120-vs-90-day reporting change and funding source.

correct_entities.why: SSA correctly identified.

bills.title: Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act of 2026

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act of 2026 (H.R. 9094) appropriates funds for the Secretary of Agriculture to provide disaster relief to states for agricultural losses caused by freezes or cold weather.

The bill appropriates $3.5 billion from the U.S. Treasury for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, as an additional amount for the "Office of the Secretary" of the U.S. Department of Agriculture. These funds would remain available until expended.

The money is to be distributed as block grants to states to compensate for losses of revenue, quality, or production of crops (including crops prevented from being planted), as well as trees, bushes, and vines, when those losses result from a freeze or cold weather condition. The assistance must include compensation to producers for losses of multi-year crops.

Eligibility for the funds is limited in two key ways. First, the funds may be used exclusively for losses occurring in counties that received a disaster designation for freeze and related cold weather conditions issued by the Secretary on March 4, 2026. Second, the funds are made available only upon the request of a state department of agriculture or another appropriate state agency as determined by the Secretary.

The bill requires the Secretary to submit a report to the Committees on Appropriations of the House and Senate describing how the assistance is being implemented. The first report is due no later than 120 days after the bill's enactment, with additional reports each fiscal quarter thereafter until the appropriated funds are expended.

The appropriated amount is designated by Congress as an emergency requirement under section 251(b)(2)(A)(i) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985.

The bill was introduced in the House on June 2, 2026, and referred to the Committee on Appropriations and the Committee on the Budget.

bills.title: Secure America Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Secure America Act (Public Law 119-98), enacted June 10, 2026, is a budget reconciliation measure that provides supplemental appropriations for fiscal year 2026 to fund border security, immigration enforcement, and related Department of Homeland Security operations. All funds appropriated remain available until September 30, 2029.

The Act is divided into two titles. Title I, under the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs, appropriates funds for functions other than immigration and customs enforcement:
- $9.55 billion to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to hire, pay, train, and equip Border Patrol agents and support personnel. None of these funds may be used to recruit, hire, or train "processing coordinators" after October 31, 2028.
- $7.45 billion to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for Homeland Security Investigations personnel and operations, of which $108.5 million is designated for additional child exploitation investigators and forensic analysts to support identifying and rescuing victims of child sexual exploitation.
- $3.45 billion to CBP for inspection equipment, air and marine platforms, border surveillance technology, the biometric entry-and-exit system, and combating drug trafficking. Funds may not be used for surveillance towers that have not been tested and accepted as delivering "autonomous" capabilities (defined as systems using AI or similar algorithms to detect and track items without continuous human control).
- $2.5 billion to the Secretary of Homeland Security for the title's purposes.

Title II, under the Committee on the Judiciary, funds immigration enforcement:
- $13.02 billion to CBP for hiring, equipping, and supporting agents to carry out immigration enforcement.
- $31.075 billion to ICE for personnel, transportation, removal operations, information technology, facilities, fleet, 287(g) agreements with state and local authorities, and legal staff. At least $350 million is designated for detainer management and arrests of "covered unlawful aliens" (defined to include certain inadmissible, deportable, or criminally charged aliens) in jurisdictions that are not "qualified cooperating jurisdictions"; Indian tribal governments are excluded from that category. These funds generally may not be used to release such aliens except as required by existing law.
- $2.5 billion to the Secretary of Homeland Security for the title's purposes.

The Secure America Act provides additional fiscal year 2026 appropriations to the Department of Homeland Security, primarily for U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) and U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), with most funds available through September 30, 2029.

The Act appropriates $9.55 billion to CBP to hire, pay, train, and equip Border Patrol agents and support personnel for functions other than immigration enforcement and customs functions. It also appropriates $3.45 billion to CBP for border security technology and screening, including nonintrusive inspection equipment, artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies, air and marine response platforms, border surveillance technology, biometric entry and exit system expenses, drug trafficking interdiction, and mission support not related to immigration or customs enforcement. Funds may not be used for surveillance towers along the southwest or northern borders unless CBP has tested and accepted them to deliver autonomous capabilities, as defined in the Act.

The Act appropriates $7.45 billion to ICE for Homeland Security Investigations personnel and mission support for functions other than immigration or customs enforcement. Of that amount, $108.5 million is designated for child exploitation investigators, forensic analysts, victim identification, rescue support, and related training.

For immigration enforcement, the Act appropriates $13.02 billion to CBP and $31.075 billion to ICE for personnel, transportation for removals, information technology, facilities, fleet needs, 287(g) agreements with state and local authorities, legal staff, and other enforcement operations. At least $350 million of ICE’s funding must support operations involving specified “covered unlawful aliens” in jurisdictions that are not “qualified cooperating jurisdictions,” with Indian tribal governments excluded from that category. The Act also provides two separate $2.5 billion appropriations to the Secretary of Homeland Security for purposes specified in the Act.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Secure America Act (Public Law 119-98) appropriates supplemental funds for fiscal year 2026, remaining available until September 30, 2029, to the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for border security, technology, personnel, and immigration enforcement. 

Under Title I, the Act provides funding for activities other than immigration and customs enforcement. This includes $9.55 billion to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) for Border Patrol personnel, with a restriction prohibiting the use of these funds for processing coordinators after October 31, 2028. It allocates $7.45 billion to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for Homeland Security Investigations, including $108.5 million specifically for child exploitation investigations and victim identification. Additionally, CBP receives $3.45 billion for border security technology, screening, and drug interdiction, with a restriction prohibiting funds for southwest or northern border surveillance towers unless they are certified as "autonomous." The Secretary of DHS receives an additional $2.5 billion for Title I purposes.

Under Title II, the Act funds direct immigration enforcement activities. It appropriates $13.02 billion to CBP and $31.075 billion to ICE. ICE’s funding supports personnel, removal operations, information technology, facility and fleet maintenance, and the expansion of 287(g) coordination agreements with state and local authorities. Notably, at least $350 million of ICE's funding must be used to arrest and manage "covered unlawful aliens" in jurisdictions that do not cooperate with federal immigration authorities, with a strict prohibition on using these funds to release such individuals. Finally, the Act appropriates an additional $2.5 billion to the Secretary of DHS for Title II or other specified purposes.

Under Title I, the Act appropriates $9.55 billion to U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) to hire, train, and equip Border Patrol agents for functions other than immigration and customs enforcement, with a restriction on hiring processing coordinators after October 31, 2028. It provides $7.45 billion to U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) for Homeland Security Investigations (HSI) agents and operations, including $108.5 million for child exploitation investigations, explicitly excluding HSI's immigration and customs enforcement missions. An additional $3.45 billion is allocated to CBP for border security technology, such as nonintrusive inspection equipment and biometric entry-exit systems, with a restriction prohibiting funds for untested surveillance towers lacking autonomous capabilities. A further $2.5 billion is appropriated to the DHS Secretary for Title I purposes.

Under Title II, the Act appropriates $13.02 billion to CBP and $31.075 billion to ICE specifically for immigration enforcement activities. ICE funding covers personnel, transportation, IT, facility and fleet maintenance, 287(g) agreements with state and local authorities, and legal advisors. It includes at least $350 million for detainer management and arrests of "covered unlawful aliens" in jurisdictions lacking a 287(g) agreement or compliance certification with federal information-sharing laws, though Indian tribal governments are exempted from this classification. These funds cannot be used to release, parole, or facilitate the community release of covered unlawful aliens encountered. Finally, $2.5 billion is appropriated to the DHS Secretary for Title II purposes or specified provisions of Public Law 119-21.

None of these funds may be used for processing coordinators after October 31, 2028. U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) receives $7.45 billion for Homeland Security Investigations agents and support personnel, also for non-immigration and non-customs enforcement functions. This includes $108.5 million specifically for additional child exploitation investigators and forensic analysts to identify and rescue victims of child sexual exploitation. CBP also receives $3.45 billion for border security technology, including nonintrusive inspection equipment, air and marine assets, surveillance technologies, biometric entry-exit systems, counter-fentanyl efforts, and other mission support not related to immigration or customs enforcement. The bill restricts funding for surveillance towers along the southwest and northern borders to only those systems that CBP has accepted as having autonomous capabilities. A final Title I appropriation provides $2.5 billion to the Secretary of Homeland Security for these same purposes.

Under Title II, CBP receives $13.02 billion specifically to carry out immigration enforcement activities, including hiring and equipping agents and support staff. ICE receives $31.075 billion for immigration enforcement, covering personnel, transportation for removals, information technology, facility and fleet maintenance, agreements with state and local authorities under section 287(g) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, and the Office of the Principal Legal Advisor. At least $350 million is directed toward arresting certain criminal aliens in jurisdictions that are not qualified cooperating jurisdictions—defined as those without a section 287(g) agreement or a filed certification of compliance with specific information-sharing laws—and prohibits using those funds to release such individuals into the community except as required by existing law. Tribal governments are excluded from being treated as non-cooperating jurisdictions. An additional $2.5 billion is appropriated to the Secretary for these Title II activities. All funds remain available through September 30, 2029, and the Act was approved on June 10, 2026.

bills.title: Aerial Firefighting Enhancement Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Aerial Firefighting Enhancement Act of 2025 (Public Law 119-18) amends the Wildfire Suppression Aircraft Transfer Act of 1996 to reauthorize and modify the Department of Defense's authority to sell aircraft and related parts for wildfire suppression purposes.

The bill makes several changes to existing law. First, it expands the permitted use of the aircraft by adding references to "water" alongside the existing references to "fire retardant," so that aircraft sold under this authority may be used to deliver water in addition to fire retardant. Second, it revises the restriction on how sold aircraft may be used, providing that such aircraft "may be used only for the provision of aircraft services for wildfire suppression purposes."

The bill also makes a technical cross-reference correction within the law and replaces the provision governing the time period during which the sale authority may be exercised. Under the new language, the authority begins on the date the Aerial Firefighting Enhancement Act of 2025 is enacted (June 12, 2025) and ends on October 1, 2035.

The Act was passed by the Senate on April 8, 2025, passed by the House on June 3, 2025, and approved on June 12, 2025.

bills.title: ACES Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The ACES Act of 2025 (Public Law 119-32) directs a study on the rates of cancer occurrence and death among former military aircrew members. Its primary purpose is to have the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine examine whether individuals who served as active duty aircrew in the Armed Forces experience elevated cancer prevalence and mortality.

The Act requires the Secretary of Veterans Affairs, within 30 days of enactment, to seek an agreement with the National Academies to conduct the study. The Secretary must finalize that agreement within 60 days of beginning negotiations. If the Secretary fails to meet this deadline, the Secretary must report to the Senate and House Committees on Veterans' Affairs explaining the failure and estimating a finalization date, and must provide progress briefings to those committees at least every 60 days thereafter.

The study must: (1) identify exposures associated with the military occupations of covered individuals, including chemicals, compounds, agents, and other phenomena; (2) review existing literature to determine associations between those exposures and overall cancer morbidity and mortality, as well as specific cancers—brain, colon and rectal, kidney, lung, melanoma skin cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, pancreatic, prostate, testicular, thyroid, urinary bladder, and other cancers the Secretary deems appropriate in consultation with the National Academies; and (3) determine, where possible, the prevalence of and mortality from those cancers among covered individuals using available data sources. Such sources may include administrative and health care databases of the Departments of Veterans Affairs and Defense and the individual Services, the National Death Index maintained by the CDC's National Center for Health Statistics, and a prior study conducted under the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021.

Upon completion, the National Academies must submit a report of the results to the Secretary and the two Veterans' Affairs committees.

A "covered individual" is defined as someone who served on active duty in the Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps as an aircrew member of a fixed-wing aircraft, including pilots, navigators, weapons systems operators, aircraft system operators, or any other crew member who regularly flew in a fixed-wing aircraft.

The Act was approved August 14, 2025.

The study must also determine, to the extent possible, the prevalence of and mortality from those cancers among covered individuals using available data sources, which may include Department of Veterans Affairs, Department of Defense, and military service databases; the National Death Index; and a prior study required by the fiscal year 2021 National Defense Authorization Act.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The ACES Act of 2025 directs the Secretary of Veterans Affairs to contract with the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine to study cancer prevalence and mortality among veterans who served on active duty as aircrew members in fixed-wing aircraft. The Secretary must begin negotiating this agreement within 30 days of the law’s August 14, 2025, enactment, and finalize it within 60 days of starting negotiations. If the Secretary misses that deadline, a report explaining the delay and an estimated completion date must be submitted to the House and Senate Committees on Veterans’ Affairs, followed by progress briefings at least every 60 days until the agreement is in place.

The required study has three main components. First, it must identify occupational exposures common to these aircrew members, including chemicals, compounds, agents, and other phenomena. Second, it must review existing scientific literature to assess whether those exposures are associated with overall cancer illness and death, and specifically with elevated rates of brain, colorectal, kidney, lung, melanoma skin, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, pancreatic, prostate, testicular, thyroid, and urinary bladder cancers, as well as any other cancers the Secretary and the National Academies deem appropriate. Third, it must determine, to the extent possible, the actual prevalence of and mortality from those cancers among covered individuals using available data sources. These may include health and administrative databases from the Department of Veterans Affairs, the Department of Defense, and individual military services; the national death index maintained by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention; and the results of a cancer study previously conducted under the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2021.

After completing the study, the National Academies must deliver a report of its findings to the Secretary and the congressional veterans’ affairs committees. For this law, a covered individual is any person who served on active duty in the Army, Navy, Air Force, or Marine Corps as an aircrew member of a fixed-wing aircraft, including pilots, navigators, weapons systems operators, aircraft system operators, and other crew members who regularly flew. The law does not amend other statutes and establishes no further regulatory or benefit deadlines.
