bills.title: Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act" (Public Law 119-77) amends Section 205(r) of the Social Security Act to improve coordination between the Social Security Administration, federal and state agencies, and the federal "Do Not Pay" working system, with the goal of reducing improper payments to deceased individuals.

The bill directs the Commissioner of Social Security to provide death information (collected under paragraph (1) of the same section) to the agency operating the Do Not Pay working system, "to the extent feasible." This data sharing is intended to help prevent improper payments and support the recovery of payments that were improperly made. The sharing must occur through a cooperative arrangement that meets existing statutory requirements (subparagraphs (A) and (B) of paragraph (3)). While data is being provided, the Commissioner and the Do Not Pay agency must enter into an agreement, based on an agreed-upon methodology, covering the proportional share of state death data costs; this agreement may be periodically reviewed.

The bill adds a new provision (paragraph (12)) barring the Commissioner from recording a death on a shareable record for any individual unless the Commissioner finds "clear and convincing evidence" supporting that the individual should be presumed deceased.

To address individuals who are incorrectly identified as deceased, the bill amends paragraph (7) to require the Commissioner, upon learning of such an error, to notify any agency that has a cooperative arrangement under paragraph (3) or (11) of the error.

These amendments take effect on December 27, 2026. The Act itself was approved on February 10, 2026.

The Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act amends the Social Security Act to improve sharing of death information between the Social Security Administration and the federal Do Not Pay working system, with the goal of helping prevent and recover improper federal benefits or other payments made on behalf of deceased individuals.

The Act requires the Commissioner of Social Security, to the extent feasible, to provide death information furnished to the Commissioner under existing law to the agency operating the Do Not Pay working system under title 31. The information may be used for the authorized purposes of that system, specifically to help prevent improper payments and support recovery of payments made improperly. This sharing must occur through a cooperative arrangement that satisfies existing statutory requirements for such arrangements.

The Act also requires the Social Security Administration and the Do Not Pay operating agency, while the data is being provided, to enter into an agreement based on an agreed methodology covering the Do Not Pay agency’s proportional share of State death data costs. The agencies may periodically review that agreement.

In addition, the Act bars the Commissioner from recording a death in a record that may be shared under this section unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the individual should be presumed deceased. It also requires the Commissioner to notify any agency with a relevant cooperative arrangement if an individual is found to have been incorrectly identified as deceased.

These amendments take effect on December 27, 2026.

The "Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act" (Public Law 119-77) aims to improve coordination between federal and state agencies and the "Do Not Pay" working system to prevent and recover improper payments made to deceased individuals. 

To achieve this, the Act amends the Social Security Act to direct the Commissioner of Social Security, to the extent feasible, to share collected death information with the agency operating the Do Not Pay working system. This sharing must occur through a cooperative arrangement that meets existing statutory requirements. Under this arrangement, the Commissioner and the Do Not Pay agency must enter into an agreement based on an agreed-upon methodology to cover the proportional share of State death data costs, which they may periodically review. 

The Act also establishes strict conditions for recording deaths. The Commissioner is prohibited from recording a death on any record shared under this section unless there is clear and convincing evidence to support the presumption that the individual is deceased. Furthermore, if an individual is incorrectly identified as deceased, the Commissioner is required to notify any agency that has a cooperative data-sharing arrangement with the Social Security Administration of the error. 

These provisions and amendments are scheduled to take effect on December 27, 2026.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, the Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act, amends the Social Security Act to strengthen coordination between the Social Security Administration (SSA) and the Do Not Pay working system, which helps federal and state agencies prevent improper payments. Its primary purpose is to reduce payments made erroneously to deceased individuals and to improve responses when living people are mistakenly marked as dead.

The bill makes two main changes. First, it authorizes the Commissioner of Social Security to provide death information received from states to the agency operating the Do Not Pay working system, to the extent feasible. This sharing must help prevent improper benefit payments and support recovering payments already made. It is subject to the same privacy and security requirements that apply to current data-sharing arrangements. The Commissioner and the Do Not Pay working system agency must enter into an agreement covering the proportional share of state death data costs, which can be reviewed periodically.

Second, the bill establishes a new procedural safeguard: SSA may not record a death for any individual in data provided under this authority unless the Commissioner determines there is clear and convincing evidence that the person should be presumed dead.

The bill also expands existing error-correction requirements. When SSA learns it incorrectly added a living person to the Death Master File or similar records, it must now notify not only the agency that originally reported the error but also any agency that has a cooperative data-sharing arrangement with SSA.

The changes take effect on December 27, 2026.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Ending Improper Payments to Deceased People Act
This act permanently allows the Department of the Treasury to access certain death records maintained by the Social Security Administration (SSA) to help prevent and recover improper payments (e.g., payments to deceased individuals). The act also establishes evidentiary requirements the SSA must meet before identifying an individual as deceased.
Current law requires the SSA to share its Death Master File with the Do Not Pay system maintained by Treasury for three years. The act makes this requirement permanent. Treasury must enter into an agreement with the SSA related to Treasury's share of the cost of state death data.
The act also prohibits the SSA from recording a death in the master file unless the SSA has clear and convincing evidence that the individual should be presumed deceased. If an individual is incorrectly identified as deceased and provides the SSA with supporting documentation, the SSA may notify certain agencies that have access to the master file, including Treasury and federal or state agencies that provide or disburse federally funded benefits.

bills.title: MATE Improvement Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Medication Access and Training Expansion Improvement Act (MATE Improvement Act) amends Section 303 of the Controlled Substances Act (21 U.S.C. 823) to clarify and expand the list of organizations and educational programs that can satisfy the training requirements for practitioners who prescribe controlled substances.

Existing law requires certain prescribers of controlled substances to complete training, and it specifies which professional organizations and accredited schools may provide qualifying training. This bill broadens those provisions in several ways:

- It adds the American Academy of Family Physicians, the American Podiatric Medical Association, the Academy of General Dentistry, and the American Optometric Association to the list of organizations whose training programs can count toward the requirement, and adds the Council on Podiatric Medical Education as a recognized accrediting body.
- It adds podiatric medicine to the categories of medical training (alongside osteopathic medicine and dental surgery) and references a podiatric medicine curriculum.
- For another category of qualifying training, it adds the American Pharmacists Association, the Accreditation Council on Pharmacy Education, the American Psychiatric Nurses Association, the American Academy of Nursing, and the American Academy of Family Physicians as recognized organizations.
- It adds accredited schools of pharmacy in the United States to the list of institutions whose curricula can satisfy the training requirement.

The bill also makes technical changes, including redesignating a duplicated subsection (the second subsection "(l)" becomes subsection "(m)").

The amendments are given retroactive effect: they take effect as if enacted on December 29, 2022, the date the underlying training requirement was originally established.

The bill was introduced in the Senate on March 11, 2025, by Mr. Bennet and Ms. Collins, and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

The MATE Improvement Act amends the Controlled Substances Act provisions governing required training for practitioners who prescribe controlled substances. Its primary purpose is to clarify and expand the types of professional training and educational programs that may satisfy existing federal training requirements related to substance use disorder treatment and controlled substance prescribing.

The bill first corrects a numbering issue in section 303 of the Controlled Substances Act by redesignating the second subsection labeled “(l)” as subsection “(m).” It then amends that subsection to add several professional organizations and accrediting bodies to the lists connected with qualifying training. These include the American Academy of Family Physicians, the American Podiatric Medical Association, the Academy of General Dentistry, the American Optometric Association, and the Council on Podiatric Medical Education. It also adds the American Academy of Family Physicians in another provision relating to qualifying training.

The bill expands qualifying educational pathways by adding podiatric medicine to provisions that already refer to allopathic medicine, osteopathic medicine, dental surgery, and dental medicine, and by including dental or podiatric medicine curricula where the law refers to relevant medical or dental curricula.

For other categories of practitioners, the bill adds the American Pharmacists Association, the Accreditation Council on Pharmacy Education, the American Psychiatric Nurses Association, the American Academy of Nursing, and the American Academy of Family Physicians to the listed organizations associated with qualifying training or accreditation. It also adds accredited schools of pharmacy to the educational institutions that may satisfy the applicable training-related requirement.

These amendments are retroactive: they take effect as if they had been enacted on December 29, 2022.

Under the bill, several organizations are newly designated as approved training providers or accrediting bodies. These include the American Academy of Family Physicians, the American Podiatric Medical Association, the Academy of General Dentistry, the American Optometric Association, the Council on Podiatric Medical Education, the American Pharmacists Association, the Accreditation Council on Pharmacy Education, the American Psychiatric Nurses Association, and the American Academy of Nursing.

Finally, the bill contains a retroactive effective date, specifying that all of these amendments shall take effect as if they were enacted on December 29, 2022.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Medication Access and Training Expansion Improvement Act or the MATE Improvement Act
This bill expands the types of organizations that may provide required training for practitioners registering with the Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) to dispense (i.e., prescribe or administer) certain controlled substances.
Current law requires health care practitioners to register with the DEA and complete a one-time training on substance use disorders from specified entities in order to dispense schedule II-V controlled substances. Practitioners who graduated within the last five years from specified types of schools and whose curriculum included similar training on substance use disorders are exempt from having to receive this additional training.
The bill adds several organizations (e.g., the American Academy of Family Physicians) to the list of entities that may provide the required one-time training for physicians or other practitioners. It also expands the types of practitioners who are exempt from this training requirement to include those who graduated from schools of podiatric medicine or schools of pharmacy within the last five years and received similar training as part of their curriculums.
The bill applies retroactively, taking effect as if enacted on December 29, 2022.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Improving Access to Addiction Medicine Providers Act" (S. 1036) is a bill introduced in the U.S. Senate that would amend Section 597 of the Public Health Service Act, which governs the Minority Fellowship Program.

The bill makes two specific changes to existing law. First, in the provision describing the program's purpose, it inserts the word "diagnosis" into the list of activities for which the program is relevant. Second, it adds "addiction medicine" to the list of professional fields—which already includes psychiatry—for which fellowships under the Minority Fellowship Program may be awarded.

The overall effect of these amendments is to authorize fellowships under the Minority Fellowship Program to be used for training professionals in the addiction medicine field.

The bill does not specify a particular effective date, and it contains no separate exceptions or conditions beyond the textual amendments described. It was introduced on March 13, 2025, by Senators Cornyn and Luján and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The "Improving Access to Addiction Medicine Providers Act" (S. 1036) is a bill that aims to expand the scope of the federal Minority Fellowship Program to include training for professionals in the field of addiction medicine. Introduced in the United States Senate on March 13, 2025, the bill proposes specific amendments to Section 597 of the Public Health Service Act.

Under current law, the Minority Fellowship Program supports the education and training of behavioral health professionals. S. 1036 modifies this program in two ways. First, it amends subsection (a)(1) of the Act to insert the word "diagnosis," after "related to," thereby incorporating diagnosis-related training into the program's objectives. Second, it amends subsection (b) to insert "addiction medicine," after "psychiatry," which explicitly authorizes the program to award fellowships to professionals training in the field of addiction medicine.

bills.title: Halting the Epidemic of Addiction and Loss Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Halting the Epidemic of Addiction and Loss Act of 2025" (S. 2336) directs the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) to ensure that references to opioid overdose reversal drugs in certain federal grant programs are not limited to naloxone specifically, but instead encompass all approved overdose reversal medications.

The bill requires the Secretary of Health and Human Services to ensure that, as appropriate, whenever HHS issues a regulation or guidance for any grant program addressing opioid misuse and use disorders, any reference to an opioid overdose reversal drug (such as a reference to naloxone) is inclusive of any opioid overdose reversal drug approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act for emergency treatment of a known or suspected opioid overdose.

The bill also addresses existing references in HHS regulations and guidance. Within one year of enactment, the Secretary must update all qualifying references to be inclusive of any opioid overdose reversal drug approved or otherwise authorized for use by the Food and Drug Administration. This updating requirement applies to references to overdose reversal drugs that were issued before the date of enactment and that appear in two specific grant programs: (1) the grant program for State and Tribal response to opioid use disorders under section 1003 of the 21st Century Cures Act (commonly known as "State Opioid Response Grants" and "Tribal Opioid Response Grants"); and (2) the grant program for priority substance use disorder prevention needs of regional and national significance under section 516 of the Public Health Service Act.

The bill was introduced in the Senate on July 17, 2025, by Senators Cornyn and Hassan, and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

For existing HHS regulations or guidance issued before enactment, the bill requires the Secretary to update covered references within one year after enactment. These updates must make references to opioid overdose reversal drugs inclusive of any such drug that has been approved or otherwise authorized for use by the Food and Drug Administration.

The requirement for updating existing references applies to HHS regulations or guidance in two specified grant programs: the State and Tribal response to opioid use disorders grant program under section 1003 of the 21st Century Cures Act, commonly known as State Opioid Response Grants and Tribal Opioid Response Grants; and the grant program for priority substance use disorder prevention needs of regional and national significance under section 516 of the Public Health Service Act. The bill may be cited as the “Halting the Epidemic of Addiction and Loss Act of 2025.”

bills.title: Safe Response Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Safe Response Act" (S. 2532) reauthorizes and updates the first responder training program established under Section 546 of the Public Health Service Act.

The bill makes several amendments to existing law. It broadens the program's scope beyond opioids by removing references to "opioid" and "opioid and heroin" throughout the statute and replacing certain terms with broader language. For example, it changes "opioid and heroin" to "opioid, heroin, and other drug," and "opioid overdose" to "overdose," expanding the program to address drug overdoses more generally rather than focusing solely on opioids.

The bill also revises the standard for the overdose-reversal drugs and devices covered by the program. Where current law refers to drugs or devices that are "approved or cleared" (by the Food and Drug Administration), the bill replaces this with "approved, cleared, or otherwise legally marketed," potentially covering a wider range of products.

Additionally, the bill makes a technical change by capitalizing "Tribes and Tribal" in subsection (a).

Finally, the bill updates the program's funding authorization. It strikes the prior authorization of $36,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2019 through 2023 and replaces it with an authorization of $57,000,000 for each of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.

The bill was introduced in the Senate on July 30, 2025, by Senators Baldwin, Hassan, and Capito, and was referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

The bill amends Section 201(d) of title II of division P of the Further Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2020 (codified at 16 U.S.C. 941h(d)) by replacing the year "2025" with "2030." This change extends the period during which funding is authorized for these Great Lakes monitoring, assessment, and research efforts through 2030.

The Act was approved on December 26, 2025. It was passed by the Senate on November 19, 2025, and by the House on December 15, 2025.

The "Great Lakes Fishery Research Reauthorization Act" (Public Law 119-67) is a federal statute enacted to reauthorize funding for monitoring, assessing, and researching the Great Lakes Basin. The primary purpose of the Act is to extend the authorization of these activities to ensure continued scientific oversight of the Great Lakes fishery resources.

To accomplish this, the Act amends existing law, specifically Section 201(d) of title II of division P of the Further Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2020 (codified at 16 U.S.C. 941h(d)). The sole legislative change made by this Act is to strike the year "2025" from the existing funding authorization and insert "2030." This modification extends the authorization of appropriations for the Great Lakes Basin monitoring, assessment, and research programs for an additional five years, through the year 2030. 

The Act contains no other provisions, exceptions, or conditions. It was approved by Congress and signed into law on December 26, 2025.

bills.title: Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025 amends titles 11 and 28 of the U.S. Code to increase compensation for chapter 7 bankruptcy trustees, adjust certain bankruptcy fees, and extend the terms of some temporary bankruptcy judgeships. The bill states that the bankruptcy system is intended to be self-funding at no cost to taxpayers, and notes that trustee compensation has not increased since 1994.

Trustee Compensation: The bill amends Section 330 of title 11 to raise one component of a trustee's per-case compensation from $45 to $105 (with the existing $15 component, this brings total compensation to $120 per case) and repeals subsection 330(e). It directs how the remainder of fees collected under section 1930(a)(1)(A) are deposited, allocating $63.51 to a Treasury special fund, $25.00 to a fund under the Deficit Reduction Act of 2005, and $51.49 to the United States Trustee System Fund. Corresponding changes are made to Section 589a of title 28.

Bankruptcy Fees: The bill modifies quarterly fees under Section 1930(a)(6)(B), extending a "5-year" reference to "10-year," and revising fee calculation formulas (including changing a 0.8 figure to 0.9). It extends related deposit provisions from 2026 to 2031 and specifies how fees are deposited for fiscal years 2026 through 2031, including depositing $5,400,000 annually into the Treasury's general fund.

Temporary Judgeships: The bill amends the Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2020 and the Bankruptcy Judgeship Act of 2017 to extend the terms of specified temporary bankruptcy judge offices from 5 years to 10 years.

The bill states it will not alter the chapter 7 filing fee and will not affect courts' authority to waive filing fees for indigent individuals.

Effective Dates: The amendments generally take effect on the first calendar quarter beginning on or after enactment. The trustee compensation changes apply to cases commenced on or after the first October 1 after enactment. The fee changes apply to cases pending or commenced, and disbursements made, on or after the first applicable quarter. The Act was approved February 6, 2025.

openai__gpt-5.5.label: openai/gpt-5.5
openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
The Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025 amends federal bankruptcy law to increase compensation for Chapter 7 bankruptcy trustees, adjust bankruptcy fee deposits and Chapter 11 quarterly fees, and extend certain temporary bankruptcy judgeships.

The Act raises the statutory payment to Chapter 7 trustees under title 11 from $45 to $105 per case. Together with the existing $15 payment, this makes the trustee’s per-case compensation $120. It also removes a prior subsection of section 330 and redirects the remaining Chapter 7 filing fee amounts: $63.51 to the judiciary’s special fund, $25 to the Deficit Reduction Act special fund, and $51.49 to the United States Trustee System Fund. The Act does not change the Chapter 7 filing fee or the authority of courts to waive filing fees for indigent individuals.

The Act amends Chapter 11 quarterly fee provisions in title 28 by extending a specified period from 5 years to 10 years and modifying the fee formula, including changing one percentage from 0.8 percent to 0.9 percent and requiring use of the greater of specified amounts in part of the calculation. For fiscal years 2026 through 2031, most Chapter 11 quarterly fees are to be deposited as provided under the amended United States Trustee System Fund rules, except that $5.4 million each fiscal year must be deposited in the general fund of the Treasury.

The Act extends the duration of multiple temporary bankruptcy judge offices authorized under the Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2020 and the Bankruptcy Judgeship Act of 2017 from 5 years to 10 years.

Except for specified provisions, the Act takes effect on the first day of the calendar quarter occurring on or after enactment. The trustee compensation changes apply to Chapter 7 cases, and cases converted to Chapter 7, commenced on or after the first October 1 after enactment. The Chapter 11 fee changes apply to cases pending or commenced on the relevant effective date and to disbursements made in calendar quarters beginning on or after enactment.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
The Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025 modifies the compensation paid to Chapter 7 bankruptcy trustees, adjusts certain bankruptcy fees, and extends temporary bankruptcy judgeships.

Specifically, the Act increases the case-by-case compensation for Chapter 7 trustees from $45 to $105. It replaces percentage-based allocations with specific dollar amounts for depositing the remainder of filing fees into the Treasury, the Deficit Reduction Act special fund, and the United States Trustee System Fund. 

Additionally, the Act extends certain quarterly fee provisions and deposit periods from five to ten years (through 2031) and adjusts quarterly fee rates. For fiscal years 2026 through 2031, it directs $5.4 million of collected quarterly fees annually to the general fund of the Treasury. The Act also extends the terms of various temporary bankruptcy judgeships, originally established under previous legislation, from 5 years to 10 years.

Generally, the Act takes effect on the first day of the first calendar quarter occurring on or after its enactment. However, the trustee compensation changes apply to Chapter 7 cases (including cases converted from other chapters to Chapter 7) commenced on or after the first October 1st following enactment. The fee adjustments apply to Chapter 11 cases pending or commenced on the first day of the first calendar quarter after enactment, as well as to quarterly fees for disbursements made in any calendar quarter beginning on or after enactment.

accurate.why: Accurate, detailed account of the Chapter 7 trustee compensation increase, the extended fees/judgeships, and the effective-date rules.

z-ai__glm-5.2.label: z-ai/glm-5.2
z-ai__glm-5.2.summary:
The Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025 amends Titles 11 and 28 of the United States Code to increase compensation for chapter 7 bankruptcy trustees, adjust bankruptcy fees, and extend the terms of certain temporary bankruptcy judgeships.

Under Section 3, the bill increases the base compensation for chapter 7 trustees from $45 to $105 per case under Section 330(b)(1) of Title 11. Combined with existing provisions, this raises total trustee compensation to $120 per case. It also eliminates subsection (e) of Section 330. The bill modifies the distribution of remaining filing fees collected under Title 28, allocating specific dollar amounts to the Treasury's special fund, the Deficit Reduction Act of 2005 fund, and the United States Trustee System Fund. It further amends Section 589a of Title 28 to adjust how funds are deposited into the United States Trustee System Fund.

Section 4 adjusts quarterly fees in chapter 11 cases by extending the fee application period from 5 to 10 years and modifying the fee percentages. It also extends the period for certain fee deposits from 2026 to 2031. For fiscal years 2026 through 2031, $5.4 million of collected quarterly fees will be deposited into the Treasury's general fund.

Section 5 extends the term of temporary bankruptcy judgeships established under the Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2020 and the Bankruptcy Judgeship Act of 2017 from 5 years to 10 years.

The amendments generally take effect on the first day of the calendar quarter occurring on or after the date of enactment. Exceptions apply to trustee compensation, which applies to chapter 7 cases (or cases converted to chapter 7) commenced on or after the first October 1 following enactment. The bankruptcy fee amendments apply to chapter 11 cases pending or commenced on the first day of the calendar quarter following enactment, and to quarterly fees for disbursements made in calendar quarters beginning on or after the enactment date.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
The Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2025 increases compensation for chapter 7 bankruptcy trustees, adjusts certain bankruptcy fees, extends temporary bankruptcy judgeships, and modifies how collected fees are distributed. 

The Act raises the per-case pay for chapter 7 trustees. Previously, trustees received $60 per case ($45 under one subsection and $15 under another). The law amends the $45 amount to $105, resulting in a total of $120 per case. It also removes a related subsection concerning trustee compensation. After trustees are paid, the remaining portion of the chapter 7 filing fee is allocated in fixed dollar amounts: $63.51 to a special fund for the courts, $25.00 to a separate special fund established by the Deficit Reduction Act of 2005, and $51.49 to the United States Trustee System Fund. Correspondingly, the law changes the United States Trustee System Fund deposit from a percentage basis to a fixed $51.49 per case and reprioritizes certain fund distributions.

The Act modifies quarterly fees paid in chapter 11 cases by extending the applicable measurement period from five to ten years and adjusting the fee rates. It also directs that, for fiscal years 2026 through 2031, $5,400,000 of the collected quarterly fees be deposited into the Treasury’s general fund, with the remainder distributed as otherwise provided. The period for making these directed quarterly fee deposits is extended to 2031.

In addition, the Act extends the term of certain temporary bankruptcy judgeships from five years to ten years, amending both the Bankruptcy Administration Improvement Act of 2020 and the Bankruptcy Judgeship Act of 2017.

The changes generally take effect on the first day of the calendar quarter beginning on or after the date of enactment (February 6, 2026). However, the increased trustee compensation applies only to cases filed on or after October 1, 2026, including cases converted to chapter 7. The quarterly fee amendments apply to chapter 11 cases commenced or pending on the effective date and to quarterly fees for disbursements made in calendar quarters starting on or after enactment. The Act does not alter the chapter 7 filing fee for debtors.

bills.title: Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The "Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act" (S. 4663) appropriates funds for the Secretary of Agriculture to provide disaster relief to agricultural producers affected by freezing or cold weather conditions.

The bill appropriates $3,500,000,000 from the Treasury for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, as an additional amount under "Agricultural Programs—Office of the Secretary." These funds would remain available until expended.

The Secretary of Agriculture would use the money to provide assistance in the form of block grants to States to compensate for losses of revenue, quality, or production of crops (including crops prevented from being planted), trees, bushes, and vines that result from a freeze or cold weather condition. The assistance must include compensation to producers for losses of multi-year crops.

The bill places several conditions on the funds. They may be used exclusively for losses occurring in counties that received a disaster designation for freeze and related cold weather conditions issued by the Secretary on March 4, 2026. The funds become available only upon the request of a State department of agriculture or other appropriate State agency, as determined by the Secretary.

The bill requires reporting: no later than 120 days after enactment, and for each fiscal quarter thereafter until the funds are fully expended, the Secretary must submit reports to the Appropriations Committees of the House and Senate describing how the assistance is being implemented.

Finally, the appropriated amount is designated by Congress as an emergency requirement under section 251(b)(2)(A)(i) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985.

The bill was introduced in the Senate on June 2, 2026, by Mr. Scott of Florida and Mrs. Moody, and referred to the Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition, and Forestry.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This bill, titled the “Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act,” appropriates $3.5 billion for the Secretary of Agriculture to issue block grants to states. Its purpose is to compensate agricultural producers for revenue, quality, or production losses caused by freezes or cold weather conditions.

The funds are provided for the fiscal year ending September 30, 2027, and remain available until spent. The assistance covers losses to crops (including crops prevented from planting), trees, bushes, and vines, and specifically includes compensation for losses of multi-year crops. This appropriation is exclusively for losses that occurred in counties the Secretary designated on March 4, 2026, as disaster areas due to freeze and related cold weather conditions.

To receive the funds, a state’s department of agriculture—or another state agency the Secretary deems appropriate—must submit a request. The bill requires the Secretary to report to the House and Senate Appropriations Committees on the implementation of the assistance. The first report is due no later than 120 days after the law is enacted, with additional reports due each fiscal quarter until the money is fully spent. The entire amount is designated as an emergency requirement under the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985.

crs_reference.label: CRS (human)
crs_reference.summary:
Florida Freeze Disaster Assistance Act
This bill provides $3.5 billion in emergency funding for the Department of Agriculture (USDA) to provide block grants to certain states for losses of revenue, quality, or production of crops (including crops prevented from planting), trees, bushes, and vines as a consequence of a freeze or cold weather conditions.
The bill specifies that the funding may only be made available for losses occurring in counties that received a disaster designation for freeze and related cold weather conditions issued by USDA on March 4, 2026. (This includes certain counties in Alabama, Florida, and Georgia.)
The bill requires the assistance provided by the bill to include compensation to producers for losses of multi-year crops.
The funds provided by this bill must be made available upon the request of a state department of agriculture or other appropriate state agency.

Senate Bill 4745, introduced in the United States Senate on June 10, 2026, by Mr. Husted and co-sponsored by Mr. Moreno, proposes to designate a specific United States Postal Service facility in Ohio. The primary purpose of the bill is to name the postal facility located at 201 East Grant Avenue in Georgetown, Ohio, as the "Ulysses S. Grant Post Office Building."

The major provision of the bill establishes this official designation. To implement this change, the legislation stipulates that any reference to this specific postal facility in any federal law, map, regulation, document, paper, or other official record of the United States must be deemed a reference to the "Ulysses S. Grant Post Office Building."

The bill does not contain any material exceptions, special conditions, or specific effective dates or deadlines for the designation. Upon introduction, S. 4745 was read twice and referred to the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.

bills.title: Declaration of Independence Reaffirmation Act of 2026

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
The Declaration of Independence Reaffirmation Act of 2026 (S. 4828) is a Senate-passed bill whose primary purpose is to formally reaffirm the Declaration of Independence as an Organic Law of the United States, in commemoration of the 250th anniversary of American independence.

The bill begins with congressional findings reviewing the history and significance of the Declaration of Independence. These findings note that the Declaration was adopted on July 4, 1776, that it is recognized among the Organic Laws of the United States, and that it declares principles such as human equality, unalienable rights endowed by a Creator, and government deriving its just powers from the consent of the governed. The findings also reference the Northwest Ordinance (adopted in 1787 and reenacted in 1789), assert that Congress has authority to recognize and carry forward foundational laws, and describe the relationship between the Declaration and the Constitution in establishing a political order in which the people are sovereign.

The bill's main operative provision states that Congress reaffirms and re-adopts the Declaration of Independence as an Organic Law of the United States and as the "enduring charter of American independence, national sovereignty, natural rights, equal citizenship, and government by consent."

The bill then sets forth the complete text of the Declaration of Independence, presented in commemoration of the 250th anniversary of American independence.

The bill does not create new programs, alter existing statutory rights or obligations, appropriate funds, or impose any deadlines or enforcement mechanisms. Its provisions are declaratory and commemorative in nature. The bill includes a short title but specifies no effective date. As reflected in the text, the Senate read the bill twice, considered it, read it a third time, and passed it on June 18, 2026.

The findings also state that the Declaration is recognized among the Organic Laws of the United States, that Congress has authority to recognize and carry forward foundational laws and principles, and that the 250th anniversary of American independence calls for reaffirmation of the Declaration. The operative provision reaffirms and re-adopts the Declaration of Independence as an Organic Law of the United States and as the enduring charter of American independence, national sovereignty, natural rights, equal citizenship, and government by consent. In commemoration of the 250th anniversary of American independence, the bill also sets forth the full text of the Declaration of Independence. bills.title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management relating to "Protection of Marine Archaeological Resources".

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, enacted as Public Law 119-3, uses Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to overturn a federal regulation.

Specifically, Congress disapproves the rule issued by the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management titled "Protection of Marine Archaeological Resources," which was published in the Federal Register on September 3, 2024 (89 Fed. Reg. 71160). Under the resolution, this rule "shall have no force or effect," meaning the regulation is nullified and cannot be implemented or enforced.

The Congressional Review Act provides a mechanism for Congress to review and reject rules issued by federal agencies. When a disapproval resolution such as this one is enacted, the targeted rule is treated as though it had never taken effect.

The resolution passed the Senate on February 25, 2025, passed the House on March 6, 2025, and was approved on March 14, 2025.

This joint resolution rescinds a requirement for lessees and operators to submit an archaeological report with any oil and gas exploration or development plan they submit to the Bureau of Ocean Energy Management (BOEM) for approval of activities proposed on the Outer Continental Shelf. (An archaeological report identifies material remains of human life or activities that are at least 50 years old and that are of archaeological interest.)

bills.title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval of the proposed export of certain defense articles to Israel.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced in the Senate, would exercise Congress's authority to disapprove a specific proposed export of defense articles to Israel.

Under the Arms Export Control Act, certain proposed arms sales and exports must be submitted to Congress for review before they can proceed, and Congress may pass a joint resolution of disapproval to block them. This resolution invokes that authority to prohibit one particular transaction.

Specifically, the resolution would prohibit the export of firearms, parts, and components controlled under Category I of the United States Munitions List, valued at $1,000,000 or more, as described in Transmittal No. DDTC 23-077. That transmittal was submitted to Congress under section 36(c) of the Arms Export Control Act and published in the Congressional Record on March 24, 2025. The specific export blocked is the sale of 20,000 Colt fully automatic carbine rifles (11.5-inch barrel, 5.56mm caliber) to M.R.D. Efram Investments Ltd. in Israel, intended for ultimate use by the Israel National Police.

The resolution addresses only this single proposed export and does not contain other provisions, exceptions, or stated effective dates.

bills.title: A joint resolution providing for congressional disapproval under chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code, of the rule submitted by the Bureau of Land Management relating to "National Petroleum Reserve in Alaska Integrated Activity Plan Record of Decision".

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution exercises Congress's authority under the Congressional Review Act (chapter 8 of title 5, United States Code) to disapprove and nullify a specific federal action taken by the Bureau of Land Management (BLM).

The action at issue is a BLM "record of decision" titled "National Petroleum Reserve in Alaska Integrated Activity Plan Record of Decision," which was issued on April 25, 2022. The resolution references a July 24, 2025 opinion letter from the Government Accountability Office, printed in the Congressional Record, which concluded that this record of decision qualifies as a "rule" subject to the Congressional Review Act.

The operative effect of the resolution is that Congress disapproves the rule, and the rule "shall have no force or effect." Under the Congressional Review Act, this means the BLM's record of decision is treated as though it never took effect.

The resolution was passed by the Senate on October 30, 2025, passed by the House on November 18, 2025, and approved (signed into law) on December 5, 2025, becoming Public Law 119-47.

The resolution contains no other provisions, exceptions, or conditions beyond the disapproval of the identified rule.

bills.title: A joint resolution to direct the removal of United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced by Senator Merkley, directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities "within or against" Iran that Congress has not authorized.

The resolution opens with findings asserting that Congress holds the sole constitutional power to declare war, that the President has a constitutional responsibility to defend the United States and its people from attack, and that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for military force against Iran. It states that using military force within or against Iran constitutes introducing U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under Section 4(a) of the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision invokes Section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act for Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985 and the expedited procedures of Section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976. Under these authorities, Congress directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless such action is explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force.

The resolution includes a rule of construction clarifying that it does not prevent the United States from:
- defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel or facilities in other nations;
- collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence—including with partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and other nations and international organizations—related to defense from threats from Iran or its proxies; or
- assisting partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and other nations, either by intercepting retaliatory attacks on their territory by Iran or its proxies, or by providing defensive materiel support for such measures.

The resolution does not specify a deadline for the removal of forces. It was referred to the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities "within or against" the Islamic Republic of Iran that Congress has not authorized.

The resolution begins with findings asserting that Congress holds the sole constitutional power to declare war, that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor enacted a specific authorization for using military force there, and that current U.S. military action constitutes the introduction of armed forces into hostilities under Section 4(a) of the War Powers Resolution. The findings quote statements from President Trump, Secretary of State Rubio, and Secretary of Defense Hegseth in early 2026 characterizing the operation as a "war," and state that as of April 10, 2026, 13 U.S. servicemembers had been killed and at least 350 wounded.

The operative section directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless such action is explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. The resolution invokes expedited congressional consideration procedures provided under Section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and Section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it prevents the United States from: defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel and facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence (including with Israel and U.S. partners and allies) related to threats from Iran or its proxies; assisting Israel and other nations in defensive measures against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, including providing defensive materiel; or providing assistance for the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

The resolution was introduced by Senator Kim on April 13, 2026, and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. It specifies no separate effective date.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced by Senator Warnock, directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

The resolution invokes Congress's constitutional power to declare war and the War Powers Resolution. Its findings state that Congress has not declared war on Iran or enacted a specific authorization for the use of military force against it. The findings describe a series of events: the United States launched "Operation Epic Fury" against the Government of Iran on February 28, 2026; American service members were killed and wounded; the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps closed the Strait of Hormuz; the Department of Defense ordered additional forces to the region; and a ceasefire took effect on April 8, 2026, after which the United States announced a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. The findings assert that this use of military force constitutes the introduction of U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under the War Powers Resolution.

Section 2 directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless such involvement is explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. The resolution relies on expedited congressional procedures provided under existing law (50 U.S.C. 1546a and Public Law 94-329).

Section 3 specifies that nothing in the resolution prevents the United States from: defending against attacks on the United States or its personnel and facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence with partners and allies, including Israel, related to threats from Iran or its proxies; assisting partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, by helping them take defensive measures or providing defensive materiel support; or providing assistance for the security and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

The resolution was read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. It specifies no separate effective date.

Senate Joint Resolution 172 directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from unauthorized hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran. The resolution states that Congress has not declared war or authorized military force against Iran, despite the launch of Operation Epic Fury on February 28, 2026, and a subsequent United States blockade of the Strait of Hormuz following an April 8, 2026 ceasefire. 

Pursuant to federal law governing the removal of forces from unauthorized hostilities, the resolution mandates that the President withdraw U.S. forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless Congress explicitly enacts a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. 

The resolution establishes several exceptions, clarifying that the directive does not prevent the United States from:
* Defending against attacks on the United States, its personnel, or its facilities in other nations;
* Collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence regarding threats from Iran or its proxies with international organizations and partner nations, such as Israel;
* Assisting partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, by supporting defensive measures or providing defensive materiel; and
* Assisting with the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

accurate.why: Accurately states the War Powers removal directive, the relevant exceptions, and this resolution's specific findings.

Under the resolution, the directive to remove forces is issued pursuant to expedited legislative procedures established in existing law. The resolution includes several exceptions, specifying that it does not prevent the United States from defending against attacks on the nation, its personnel, or its facilities abroad. It also permits intelligence collection, analysis, and sharing related to Iranian threats, including with allies such as Israel. Additionally, the resolution allows the United States to assist partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, by supporting their defensive measures or providing defensive materiel. Finally, it permits assistance for the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran that lack explicit congressional authorization. It relies on Congress’s constitutional power to declare war and the War Powers Resolution, citing that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor passed a specific statutory authorization for the use of military force against it.

The bill’s findings reference Operation Epic Fury, launched February 28, 2026, as introducing U.S. forces into hostilities against the Government of Iran. It notes U.S. casualties, the closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, subsequent economic disruption, and the deployment of additional U.S. forces. It also acknowledges a ceasefire that took effect April 8, 2026, followed by a U.S.-announced blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

The operative provision, under expedited procedures set out in the Department of State Authorization Act and the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act, directs the President to remove U.S. forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless Congress explicitly authorizes such action through a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force.

A rule of construction clarifies what the resolution does not prevent: defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel and facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence on threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel and international organizations; assisting partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, with defensive measures and defensive materiel support; and aiding the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced in the Senate by Mr. Van Hollen, directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

The resolution's findings state that under Article I of the Constitution, Congress holds the sole power to declare war, and that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for military force there. The findings describe that the Trump Administration launched "Operation Epic Fury" against Iran on February 28, 2026, that the President notified Congress under the War Powers Resolution on March 2, 2026, and that as of April 8, 2026, 13 U.S. service members had been killed and 380 wounded. The findings characterize the use of military force against Iran as an introduction of U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under section 4(a) of the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless such action is explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. The resolution invokes expedited congressional consideration procedures under section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction clarifying that it does not prevent the United States from: defending against attacks on the U.S. or its personnel and facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence, including with partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026; assisting such partner countries in intercepting retaliatory attacks or providing defensive materiel support; or providing assistance for the security and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

The resolution specifies no separate effective date.

The resolution relies on an existing statutory mechanism (section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, codified at 50 U.S.C. 1546a) that provides expedited legislative procedures for resolutions requiring the removal of forces from hostilities that lack congressional authorization. It cites a series of findings, including that Congress has not declared war on Iran or authorized military force against it, that the Trump Administration launched Operation Epic Fury in February 2026, and that the fighting has already resulted in U.S. casualties.

The directive is subject to several exceptions. It does not prevent the United States from taking action to defend against an attack on the country or its personnel and facilities in other nations. It allows continued intelligence collection, analysis, and sharing with partner nations attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and with other nations and international organizations, regarding threats from Iran or its proxies. The resolution also permits assistance to attacked partner countries in intercepting retaliatory attacks on their territory and in providing defensive materiel support for such measures. Finally, it allows assistance for the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities. The resolution contains no specific effective date or deadline beyond the directive it issues.

This removal is invoked under the expedited procedures referenced in Section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and Section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution was read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations. It specifies no separate effective date.

deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to withdraw U.S. armed forces from hostilities in or against Iran that lack explicit congressional authorization. It invokes the expedited procedures set out in the Department of State Authorization Act (50 U.S.C. 1546a) and the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution begins with findings stating that Congress has not declared war or enacted a specific authorization for military force against Iran, and that on February 28, 2026, the administration launched Operation Epic Fury—described by the President and the Secretary of Defense as a war—resulting in significant U.S. military involvement, including over 50,000 participating personnel, seven combat deaths, and hundreds wounded. The findings also note that the operation led to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps closing the Strait of Hormuz, causing a historic disruption to global energy supplies, oil price spikes, and projections of widespread food insecurity.

The operative section directs the President to remove U.S. armed forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless a declaration of war or a specific statutory authorization for the use of military force is enacted. The resolution does not set a deadline for removal beyond the immediate effect of the directive.

A rule of construction clarifies that the resolution does not prevent the United States from: (1) defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel and facilities in other nations; (2) collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence related to threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel and partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026; or (3) assisting Israel and those partner countries by helping them directly defend against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, or by providing defensive materiel support for such defensive measures. No other exceptions are included.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution, introduced by Senator Booker in the Senate on April 13, 2026, invokes the War Powers Resolution to direct the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that Congress has not authorized.

The resolution begins with findings asserting that Congress holds the sole constitutional power to declare war, and that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for military force there. The findings describe a U.S. military buildup in the Middle East during early 2026 and a campaign called "Operation Epic Fury," launched February 28, 2026, in which the President ordered air and missile strikes inside Iran without congressional authorization or consultation. The findings cite figures including more than 50,000 U.S. personnel participating in the operation, and 13 service members killed and more than 350 injured by Iranian retaliatory strikes as of April 13, 2026. The findings state that this constitutes the introduction of U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under section 4(a) of the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision directs the President, pursuant to the expedited procedures referenced in section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act for Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985 and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976, to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force.

The resolution includes a rule of construction clarifying that nothing in it prevents the United States from: defending against attacks on the United States or its personnel or facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence regarding threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel and other partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026; or assisting such countries in directly defending against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, including by providing defensive materiel support.

openai__gpt-5.5.summary:
This joint resolution would direct the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless Congress explicitly authorizes those hostilities through a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force.

The resolution states congressional findings that Congress has the constitutional power to declare war, that the President has responsibility to defend the United States and its personnel from attack, and that Congress has not declared war on Iran or enacted a specific authorization for military force within or against Iran. It cites the War Powers Resolution’s purposes and consultation requirements and asserts that U.S. military operations in Iran beginning with “Operation Epic Fury” on February 28, 2026, constitute hostilities under the War Powers Resolution. The findings also describe the scale of U.S. deployments, Iranian and proxy retaliatory strikes, U.S. casualties, and economic effects following the operation.

The operative provision invokes section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act for Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985 and the expedited procedures of section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976. It directs removal of U.S. forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless Congress authorizes them.

The resolution specifies that it should not be construed to prevent the United States from defending against attacks on the United States or its personnel or facilities in other countries; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence related to threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel, partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and other nations or international organizations; or assisting Israel and such partner countries in directly defending against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, including by providing defensive materiel support. The resolution does not set a separate deadline or effective date.

google__gemini-3.5-flash.label: google/gemini-3.5-flash
google__gemini-3.5-flash.summary:
Senate Joint Resolution 183 directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress. Pursuant to the War Powers Resolution and the Department of State Authorization Act, the resolution mandates this removal unless Congress explicitly authorizes the military action through a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. 

The resolution includes several material exceptions. It specifies that the directive does not prevent the United States from:
* Defending itself, its personnel, or its facilities in other nations against attacks;
* Collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence regarding threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel and other partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026; or
* Assisting Israel, partner countries, and other nations by providing defensive materiel support or helping them directly defend against retaliatory attacks on their territories by Iran or its proxies.

The resolution's findings state that the military action, designated as "Operation Epic Fury," began on February 28, 2026, without congressional authorization or consultation, and has involved air and missile strikes inside Iran as well as the deployment of more than 50,000 U.S. service members. The resolution utilizes expedited congressional procedures to direct the withdrawal of these forces.

accurate.why: Accurately states the War Powers removal directive, the relevant exceptions, and this resolution's specific findings.

The resolution cites Congress's constitutional power to declare war and notes that Congress has not declared war on Iran or authorized military force against it. It references the War Powers Resolution and details a military buildup and "Operation Epic Fury," launched by President Trump on February 28, 2026, which involved air and missile strikes inside Iran without congressional authorization. The findings also note subsequent Iranian retaliatory strikes, U.S. military casualties, additional troop deployments, and global economic impacts.

Under the resolution's provisions, the removal of armed forces is to be carried out pursuant to expedited procedures established under existing law. However, the resolution includes specific exceptions, stating that nothing in it may be construed to prevent the United States from defending against an attack on the United States, its personnel, or its facilities in other nations. It also does not prevent the collection, analysis, or sharing of intelligence related to threats from Iran or its proxies, including with Israel and partner countries attacked since February 28, 2026. Additionally, the resolution permits the United States to assist Israel, partner countries, and other nations in directly defending against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, or by providing defensive materiel support for such defensive measures.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities "within or against" the Islamic Republic of Iran that Congress has not authorized. It invokes Congress's constitutional power to declare war and the framework of the War Powers Resolution of 1973.

The resolution's findings state that the President notified Congress on March 2, 2026, of the initiation of military force against Iran on February 28, 2026, but that Congress has neither declared war on Iran nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for the use of military force there. The findings note that, under the War Powers Resolution, the President must terminate the use of armed forces within 60 days of initial notification absent a declaration of war, a specific authorization, an extension by law, or Congress being physically unable to meet. The findings state that this 60-day period ends on May 1, 2026, and that the President may obtain only one additional 30-day extension upon certifying unavoidable military necessity related to the safety of U.S. forces.

The operative section directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for the use of military force. The resolution is brought under expedited procedures referenced in section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it prevents the United States from: defending against attacks on the U.S. or its personnel and facilities abroad; collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence (including with Israel and other partners) related to threats from Iran or its proxies; assisting Israel and other nations in defensive measures, including providing defensive materiel support; or assisting in the security, departure, and evacuation of U.S. citizens affected by the hostilities.

Under the War Powers Resolution, the 60-day statutory limit for unauthorized military force expires on May 1, 2026. Accordingly, this resolution mandates the removal of U.S. forces unless Congress explicitly declares war or provides specific statutory authorization for the use of military force.

The bill also permits the U.S. to assist Israel and other nations by providing defensive materiel and supporting defensive measures against retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies. Finally, the directive does not restrict the U.S. from providing security, departure, and evacuation assistance to American citizens affected by the hostilities.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran that have not been authorized by Congress.

The resolution begins with findings asserting that Congress holds the sole constitutional power to declare war, while acknowledging the President's constitutional responsibility to defend the United States, its territories, citizens, servicemembers, and diplomats from attack. It states that Congress has not declared war on Iran or any person or organization within Iran, nor enacted a specific statutory authorization for military force there. It characterizes the use of military force within or against Iran—including enforcing a naval blockade—as the introduction of U.S. Armed Forces into hostilities under the War Powers Resolution.

The operative provision directs the President to remove U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities within or against Iran unless those hostilities are explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force. The resolution invokes expedited congressional procedures under section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The resolution includes a rule of construction stating that nothing in it prevents the United States from: (1) defending against an attack on the United States or its personnel or facilities in other nations; (2) collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence about threats from Iran or its proxies with partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and other nations and international organizations; and (3) assisting such partner countries and other nations by helping intercept retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies or by providing defensive materiel support for those defensive measures.

The resolution was introduced on April 27, 2026, by Senator Kaine and others, referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations, and on May 19, 2026, the committee was discharged and the resolution placed on the calendar. The text specifies no further effective date.

It also states that using military force within or against Iran, including enforcing a naval blockade, constitutes the introduction of U.S. forces into hostilities under the War Powers Resolution.

The resolution relies on existing expedited procedures under section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985, and section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976, which govern consideration of measures requiring removal of U.S. forces from unauthorized hostilities.

The removal directive is subject to stated limitations. The resolution does not prevent the United States from defending against attacks on the United States or its personnel or facilities in other countries. It also does not prevent collecting, analyzing, or sharing intelligence with partner countries attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, or with other nations and international organizations as appropriate, regarding threats from Iran or its proxies. deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.label: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro
deepseek__deepseek-v4-pro.summary:
This joint resolution directs the President to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities within or against the Islamic Republic of Iran unless Congress has declared war or enacted a specific statutory authorization for such use of military force.

The resolution relies on Congress’s exclusive constitutional power to declare war and notes that neither a declaration of war nor a specific authorization for military force against Iran has been enacted. It invokes Section 1013 of the Department of State Authorization Act, Fiscal Years 1984 and 1985 (which provides expedited procedures for measures requiring the removal of forces from hostilities absent congressional authorization), and the related procedures of Section 601(b) of the International Security Assistance and Arms Export Control Act of 1976.

The core provision is a statutory directive: the President must withdraw U.S. forces from hostilities in or against Iran unless explicitly authorized by a declaration of war or a specific authorization for use of military force. The resolution does not set a fixed deadline but mandates removal.

The resolution includes a rule of construction clarifying what it does not prohibit. It explicitly preserves the ability of the United States to: (1) defend against an attack on the United States or its personnel and facilities located in other nations; (2) collect, analyze, or share intelligence with partner countries that have been attacked by Iran since February 28, 2026, and with other nations and international organizations, concerning threats from Iran or its proxies; and (3) assist partner countries that have been attacked by Iran since that date—either by helping intercept retaliatory attacks by Iran or its proxies, or by providing defensive materiel support for such defensive measures.

The resolution does not amend any existing law; it exercises authority already provided by statute to direct the removal of forces from an unauthorized engagement. It specifies no new deadlines, exemptions, or reporting requirements beyond the removal directive and its defensive carve‑outs.

bills.title: A joint resolution proposing an amendment to the Constitution of the United States relative to the fundamental right to vote.

anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.label: anthropic/claude-opus-4.8
anthropic__claude-opus-4.8.summary:
This joint resolution proposes an amendment to the United States Constitution establishing a fundamental right to vote. To take effect, the proposed amendment must be passed by two-thirds of both the Senate and the House of Representatives and then ratified by the legislatures of three-fourths of the states.

The proposed amendment contains four sections. Section 1 states that every U.S. citizen of legal voting age shall have the fundamental right to vote in any public election held in the jurisdiction where the citizen resides.

Section 2 provides that this right shall not be denied or abridged by the United States, any state, or any political subdivision within a state, unless the denial or abridgment furthers a "compelling governmental interest" and is the "least restrictive means" of furthering that interest. This establishes a legal standard that governments would have to meet to justify any limitation on voting rights.

Section 3 repeals a portion of Section 2 of the Fourteenth Amendment—specifically the phrase "or other crime." That existing provision currently addresses the reduction of a state's congressional representation when the right to vote is denied to eligible male inhabitants, except for participation in rebellion "or other crime."

Section 4 grants Congress the power to enforce the article and to protect against any denial or abridgment of the right to vote through legislation.

The resolution does not specify a deadline for ratification by the states.
